blocks:
Ayahs

يسٓ (١)
Ya Seen! (1)
وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
By the Wise Quran, (2)
إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٣)
you are indeed one of the apostles, (3)
عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤)
on a straight path. (4)
تَنزِيلَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٥)
[It is a scripture] sent down gradually from the All-mighty, the All-merciful (5)
لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أُنذِرَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٦)
that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, so they are oblivious. (6)
لَقَدْ حَقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَىٰٓ أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٧)
The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not have faith. (7)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ أَغْلَـٰلًا فَهِىَ إِلَى ٱلْأَذْقَانِ فَهُم مُّقْمَحُونَ (٨)
Indeed We have put iron collars around their necks, which are up to the chins, so their heads are upturned. (8)
وَجَعَلْنَا مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٩)
And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, then We have blind-folded them, so they do not see. (9)
وَسَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠)
It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. (10)
إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَخَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَأَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ (١١)
You can only warn someone who follows the Reminder and fears the All-beneficent in secret; so give him the good news of forgiveness and a noble reward. (11)
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا۟ وَءَاثَـٰرَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ فِىٓ إِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٢)
Indeed it is We who revive the dead and write what they have sent ahead and their effects [which they left behind], and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam. (12)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the three fundamental principles (الأصول الثلاثة) of the religion. It begins with prophethood (النبوة) and describes the state of people in accepting or rejecting the call, noting that the goal of the true call is to give life to a people by setting them upon the path of felicity (صراط السعادة) and to realize the word [of punishment] against others. In other words, it concerns the perfecting of human beings in the two paths of felicity and wretchedness.

The Surah then transitions to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — by enumerating a series of signs of His oneness. It then moves to the mention of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the final gathering of souls for judgment — mentioning the raising of people for recompense, the distinction of the guilty from the God-conscious (المتقين) on that day, and describing the ultimate fate of each group.

Then, it returns to its starting point, summarizing the discourse on the three principles and providing evidence for them, at which point the Surah concludes.

Among the most luminous verses (غرر الآيات) therein is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things, and to whom you shall be brought back." (36:82-83). Thus, the Surah is of great significance, gathering the foundations and roots of truths. It has been narrated through both Sunni and Shi'a channels that "Everything has a heart, and the heart of the Qur'an is Ya Sin." (1) The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: يس وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ (Ya Seen. By the wise Quran) until His saying فَهُمْ غافِلُونَ (so they are oblivious) is an oath by Him, the Exalted, by the Wise Qur'an regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being among the messengers. The Qur'an is described as "Wise" (الحكيم) because wisdom (الحكمة) is firmly established within it; this wisdom consists of the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف) and what branches out from them of laws (الشرائع), lessons (العبر), and exhortations (المواعظ).

His saying: إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ (you are indeed one of the apostles) is the subject of the oath (مقسم عليه), as previously mentioned.

His saying: عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ (on a straight path) is a subsequent predicate (خبر بعد خبر) for His saying "Indeed you" (إِنَّكَ). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "path" (صراط) — as has been said — is to indicate magnification (التفخيم), and its description as "straight" (المستقيم) is for clarification, for the sirat is the clear and straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah. What is meant is the path that brings those who traverse it to Allah, the Exalted; that is, to human felicity, which contains the perfection of servitude (العبودية) to Allah and proximity to Him. Some relevant discussion has already been presented in the commentary on al-Fatiha.

His saying: تَنْزِيلَ الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ ([it is] a revelation of the All-mighty, the All-merciful) is a description of the Qur'an, detached from its adjectival position and rendered in the accusative case for praise (منصوب على المدح). The infinitive (المصدر) here carries the meaning of the passive participle (المفعول). The sum of the meaning is: I mean by the Qur'an that revealed book which Allah, the All-mighty (العزيز), the All-merciful (الرحيم), has sent down, and in which might and mercy are established.

The conclusion with these two attributes is to indicate that He is the Subduer (قاهر) who is never subdued, and the Victor (غالب) who is never vanquished. Thus, the turning away of those who reject His servitude does not frustrate Him, nor does the denial of the deniers or the mendacity of the liars humiliate Him. Furthermore, He is the possessor of vast mercy for those who follow the Reminder (الذكر) and fear Him in the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — not for any benefit He gains from their faith, but to guide them to that which contains their felicity and perfection. Thus, by His might and mercy, He sent the Messenger and revealed to him the Wise Qur'an to warn the people, so that the word of punishment might be realized against some of them, while mercy encompasses others.

His saying: لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أُنْذِرَ آباؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غافِلُونَ (that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, so they are oblivious) is the rationale (تعليل) for the sending and the revelation. The word "not" (ما) is negatory, and the sentence is an attribute for "a people" (قَوْماً). The meaning is: We have only sent you and revealed the Qur'an to you so that you may warn and frighten a people whose fathers were not warned, and thus they are oblivious (غافلون).

If the "people" (القوم) refers to the Quraysh and those associated with them, then "their fathers" refers to their immediate ancestors, for among their distant ancestors was the Prophet Ishmael (عليه السلام), the sacrifice of Allah (ذبيح الله), and other messengers were sent to the Arabs such as Hud, Salih, and Shu'ayb (عليه السلام). If the "people" refers to all contemporary humanity in view of the universality of the message, the same applies, for the last messenger known for his message before him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was Jesus (عليه السلام), and between them was the period of cessation (زمان الفترة).

Know that what we have mentioned regarding the structure of the verses is what first comes to the understanding. Others have offered different interpretations that are far-fetched, which we have omitted; whoever desires them may consult the more extensive works.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not have faith) — the letter lam is for an oath, meaning: I swear that the word has become established and mandatory against most of them. What is meant by the "establishment of the word" against them is their becoming instances (مصاديق) to which the word applies.

The "word" (القول) that has become due against them is the word of punishment (كلمة العذاب) which Allah, the Glorified, spoke at the beginning of creation when addressing Iblis: فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "‘This is the truth, and the truth I say: I will surely fill hell with you and with all of those who follow you.’" (38:84-85). What is meant by "following Iblis" is obeying him in what he commands through whispering (الوسوسة) and enticement (التسويل), such that error (الغواية) becomes established and rooted in the soul, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, addressing Iblis: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "‘As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all.’" (15:42-43).

The necessary consequence of this is rebellion (الطغيان) and arrogance (الاستكبار) against the truth, as indicated by what Allah narrates of the questioning between the followed and the followers in the Fire: بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنا قَوْلُ رَبِّنا إِنَّا لَذائِقُونَ فَأَغْوَيْناكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ "‘Rather you were a rebellious people. So our Lord’s word has become due against us: we shall indeed taste [the punishment]. So we led you astray; we were ourselves perverse.’" (37:30-32), and His saying: وَلكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ قِيلَ ادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ "‘But the word of punishment has become due against the faithless.’ It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the abode of the arrogant!’" (39:71-72).

The necessary consequence of this is a total devotion to the world and a complete turning away from the Hereafter, and the rooting of this in their souls. The Exalted said: وَلكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً فَعَليهمْ غَضَبٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصارِهِمْ وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ "but those who open their breasts to unfaith, upon them shall be Allah’s wrath, and there is a great punishment for them. That is because they preferred the life of this world to the Hereafter, and because Allah does not guide the faithless people. They are the ones on whose hearts, hearing, and sight Allah has set a seal, and it is they who are the oblivious." (16:106-108). Thus, Allah sets a seal (طبع) upon their hearts, and one of its effects is that they have no way to faith. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Indeed those against whom your Lord’s word has become due will not believe" (10:96).

From what has preceded, it is clear that the "so" (الفاء) in His saying فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (so they will not have faith) is for derivation (التفريع), not for causation (التعليل) as some have supposed.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ أَغْلالاً فَهِيَ إِلَى الْأَذْقانِ فَهُمْ مُقْمَحُونَ (Indeed We have put iron collars around their necks, which reach up to their chins, so their heads are forced up) — الأعناق (necks) is the plural of unuq (عنق), meaning the neck. الأغلال (iron collars) is the plural of ghill (غل), which is said to be that with which the hand is bound to the neck for punishment and restriction. مقمحون (forced up) is the passive participle of iqmah (الإقماح), which is the raising of the head, as if the collars have filled the space between their chests and their chins, so their heads remain raised toward the sky, unable to lower them to look at the path before them to recognize it and distinguish it from others.

The indefiniteness of "collars" (أَغْلالاً) is for magnification (التفخيم) and terrorization (التهويل).

The verse serves as the rationale (تعليل) for the previous saying: فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (so they will not have faith).

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْناهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُبْصِرُونَ (And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, then We have blindfolded them, so they do not see) — السد (barrier) is the partition between two things. His saying "before them and behind them" (مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ) is a metaphor for all directions. الغشي and الغشيان (covering) means to envelop; it is said "such a thing covered him" meaning it enveloped him, and "the matter covered so-and-so" meaning it made the matter envelop him. The verse completes the previous rationale, and His saying "We have put" (جَعَلْنا) is coordinated (معطوف) with the previous "We have put."

It is narrated from al-Razi in his commentary regarding the meaning of the simile in the two verses that the obstacle to observing the signs is of two types: one that prevents looking at the souls (الأنفس), so this was likened to the collar that makes its owner forced up (مقمحاً), unable to see himself or cast his sight upon his own body; and one that prevents looking at the horizons (الآفاق), so this was likened to the surrounding barrier, for one surrounded by a barrier cannot cast his sight upon the horizons, so the signs therein do not appear to him. Whoever is afflicted by both is entirely deprived of observation.

The meaning of the two verses is that they do not believe because We have placed collars around their necks with which We bind their hands to their necks, reaching up to their chins, so their heads are raised and they remain in that state; and We have placed a barrier from all their directions, making it cover them so they do not see and thus are not guided.

In the two verses, there is a representation (تمثيل) of their state in being deprived of guidance to faith and His prohibition of that for them as a recompense for their disbelief, error, and rebellion in that regard.

It has already been mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable" (2:26) in the first volume of this book, that these descriptions and their likes in the Qur'an, with which the believers and the disbelievers are described, reveal another life for the human being within the interior of this worldly life, hidden from material sense, which will appear to him when the realities are unveiled by death or resurrection. Accordingly, the discourse in such verses follows the course of reality (الحقيقة) rather than metaphor (المجاز), contrary to what the [general] people hold.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith) is an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير) and confirmation of what the three preceding verses contain, a summary of the intended meaning, and a preparation for what follows in His saying: إِنَّما تُنْذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِّكْرَ (You can only warn him who follows the Reminder).

It is possible that it is coordinated with His saying لا يُبْصِرُونَ (they do not see), meaning: they do not see, and your warning or lack thereof is equal to them; they will not believe. The first view is closer to the understanding.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما تُنْذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِّكْرَ وَخَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَأَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ (You can only warn him who follows the Reminder and fears the All-beneficent in the Unseen. So give him the good news of forgiveness and a noble reward) — the restriction (القصر) is for isolation (للإفراد). What is meant by "warning" is the beneficial warning that has an effect. By "the Reminder" (الذكر) is meant the Noble Qur'an. By "following it" is meant believing in it and inclining toward it when its verses are recited. The use of the past tense indicates the certainty of occurrence. What is meant by "fearing the All-beneficent in the Unseen" (خشي الرحمن بالغيب) is fearing Him, the Exalted, from behind the veil and before the unveiling of reality by death or resurrection. It has been said: meaning in his absence from people, unlike the hypocrite, but this is far-fetched.

Fear (الخشية) has been linked to the name "the All-beneficent" (الرحمن), which indicates the attribute of mercy that draws hope, to signal that their fear is a dread mixed with hope; it is this which establishes the servant in the station of servitude (العبودية), so he neither feels [falsely] secure nor despairs.

The indefiniteness of "forgiveness" (بِمَغْفِرَةٍ) and "a noble reward" (أَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ) is for magnification; i.e., give him the good news of a great forgiveness from Allah and a noble reward whose value cannot be estimated, which is Paradise. The evidence for all the above is the context.

The meaning is: You only warn with the beneficial warning that has an effect him who follows the Qur'an when its verses are recited to him and inclines toward it, and fears the All-beneficent with a fear mixed with hope; so give him the good news of a great forgiveness and a noble reward whose value cannot be estimated.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِ الْمَوْتى وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ (Indeed it is We who revive the dead, and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps, and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam) — what is meant by "reviving the dead" is reviving them for recompense.

What is meant by "what they have sent ahead" (ما قدموا) are the deeds they performed before death, thus sending them ahead of their death. What is meant by "their footsteps" (آثارهم) is what they left behind after their death of good that is acted upon — such as teaching knowledge that is benefited from, building a mosque in which prayer is offered, or a place for ablution — or evil that is acted upon — such as establishing an innovated practice (سنة مبتدعة) that is followed or building a place of vice in which Allah is disobeyed.

It is sometimes said that what is meant by "what they have sent ahead" are intentions, and by "their footsteps" are the deeds resulting and branching from them, but this is far from the context.

What is meant by "writing what they have sent ahead and their footsteps" is recording them in the scrolls of their deeds and preserving them therein through the angels who record deeds. This writing is different from the writing of deeds and their enumeration in the Manifest Imam (الإمام المبين), which is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), although some have imagined that the writing of what they sent ahead and their footsteps is their enumeration in the Manifest Book. This is because He, the Exalted, establishes in His speech a Book that enumerates everything, then for every nation a Book that enumerates their deeds, then for every human being a Book that enumerates his deeds, as He said: وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ "nor anything fresh or withered, but it is in a manifest Book" (6:59), and He said: كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا "Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28), and He said: وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open" (17:13). The apparent meaning of the verse also necessitates a kind of distinction between the Book of Deeds and the Manifest Imam, as He distinguished between them by particularity and generality, and by the difference in expression between "writing" (الكتابة) and "figuring/enumerating" (الإحصاء).

His saying: وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ (and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam) — this is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), protected from change, which contains the detail of His decree, the Glorified, regarding His creation, so it enumerates everything. It has been mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, by different names such as the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب), the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين), and the Manifest Imam (الإمام المبين), each with a specific consideration.

Perhaps the consideration in naming it a "Manifest Imam" (إماماً مبيناً) is that, because it contains the definitive decree (القضاء المحتوم), it is followed by creation and is an exemplar for them. The Books of Deeds — as will come in the commentary on Surah al-Jathiyah — are transcribed from it. The Exalted said: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ "This is Our record; it speaks the truth against you. Indeed We used to record what you used to do." (45:29).

It has been said: what is meant by the Manifest Imam are the scrolls of deeds, but this is nothing. It has also been said: His knowledge, the Exalted; this is like the previous one, though if "active knowledge" (العلم الفعلي) were meant, it would have some basis.

Among the strange things said in this regard is what some have mentioned: that what is written in the Preserved Tablet is what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection, not the events of the world for all eternity. This is because the Tablet, according to Muslims, is a body (جسم), and every body is finite in dimensions as evidenced by proofs; and the explanation of everything in it in the manner known to us all at once would necessitate that the finite be a container for the infinite, which is intuitively impossible. Thus, the way is to specify the generality of "everything" and say that what is meant are the events until the Day of Resurrection.

This is an arbitrary assertion (تحكم), and we shall address it in detail.

The verse serves as a rationale (تعليل) in relation to what preceded it, as if He, the Exalted, is saying: What We have informed of and described regarding the state of those against whom the word has become due, and these who follow the Reminder and fear their Lord in the Unseen, is so because the matter of the life of all is with Us, and their deeds and footsteps are preserved with Us; thus We have knowledge and expertise regarding the ultimate fate of each of the two groups.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَهُمْ مُقْمَحُونَ (so their heads are forced up), he said: "They have raised their heads."

In the same work, in a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْناهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُبْصِرُونَ (And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, then We have blindfolded them, so they do not see) — "Guidance; Allah took away their hearing, their sight, and their hearts — and their deeds from guidance."

"It was revealed concerning Abu Jahl ibn Hisham and a group of his household — that was when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood to pray — and Abu Jahl, may Allah curse him, had sworn that if he saw him praying, he would crush his head (ليدمغه) (1) — so he came to him with a stone while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was standing in prayer — and every time he raised the stone to throw it — Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, fixed his hand to his neck and the stone would not move from his hand — so when he returned to his companions, the stone fell from his hand."

"Then another man stood up, also from his clan, and said, 'I will kill him' — so when he drew near to him, he began to hear the recitation of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — and he was terrified and returned to his companions and said — 'Something like a stallion shaking its tail came between me and him — so I was afraid to step forward.'"

"And His saying, the Exalted: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith) — so not one of that group from Banu Makhzum believed."

I say: Something similar is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il from Ibn Abbas, which states: "A group of people from Banu Makhzum conspired against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to kill him — among them Abu Jahl and al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah — while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was standing in prayer, they heard his recitation — so they sent al-Walid to him to kill him — so he went until he reached the place where he was praying — and he began to hear his recitation but could not see him, so he went back to them — and informed them of that, so they came to him — and when they reached the place where he was praying — they heard his recitation and would go toward it — but they would then hear it from behind them — so they turned away and found no way to him. That is His saying: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا (And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them) the verse."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to recite in the Mosque and would recite loudly — until some people from Quraysh were annoyed by it, so they stood up to seize him — and suddenly their hands were bound to their necks and they could not see — so they came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'We adjure you by Allah and the ties of kinship, O Muhammad' — and there was no clan of Quraysh — except that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had kinship among them — so the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) supplicated until that left them, and there was revealed: يس وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ (Ya Seen. By the wise Quran) until His saying أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (or do not warn them, they will not have faith)." He said: "So not one of that group believed."

I say: They have narrated the story in various forms; in some, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited the verses and was veiled from them so they did not see him, and Allah repelled their evil and plot from him. In some, the verses — from the beginning of the Surah until His saying: فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (so they will not have faith) — were revealed concerning the story, so His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا (Indeed We have put) until the end of the two verses recounts Allah's action with them in veiling the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from their sight, and His saying: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ (It is the same to them) etc., informs of the lack of faith of that group.

You are well aware that the context (سياق) of the verses refuses to be applied to these narrations with the story they contain. It is a consistent and harmonious context that describes the state of two groups of people: those against whom the word has become due, so they do not believe; and those who follow the Reminder and fear their Lord in the Unseen.

How far is that from interpreting His saying: لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى أَكْثَرِهِمْ (The word has certainly become due against most of them) as referring to the people being warned, and interpreting His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ (Indeed We have put iron collars around their necks) and جَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا (And We have put a barrier before them) the two verses, as referring to the story of Abu Jahl and his group, and interpreting His saying: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ (It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them) as referring to his group? Add to that interpreting His saying: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ (and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps) as referring to the story of a group of the Ansar in Medina — and its report will soon reach you — for by this the context would be disrupted and the unity of the arrangement would be fractured.

The truth is that the verses were revealed all at once with a single context, describing the state of people and their division into two groups upon the arrival of the call and the occurrence of the warning. There is no obstacle to the story having occurred and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being veiled from his enemies by the verses.

In the same work, Abd al-Razzaq, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it hasan), al-Bazzar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who graded it sahih), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri who said: "Banu Salama were in a corner of Medina — so they wanted to move near the Mosque, so Allah revealed: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِ الْمَوْتى وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ (Indeed it is We who revive the dead, and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps) — so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called them and said: 'Indeed your footsteps are being written,' then he recited the verse to them, so they desisted."

In the same work, al-Faryabi, Ahmad in al-Zuhd, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The dwellings of the Ansar were far from the Mosque — so they wanted to move near the Mosque, so there was revealed وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ (and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps) — so they said: 'Rather, we shall remain in our place.'"

I say: The discussion regarding these two narrations is the same as the discussion regarding those that preceded them.

In the same work, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Jarir ibn Abdillah al-Bajali who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Whoever establishes a good practice (سنة حسنة), he shall have its reward — and the reward of whoever acts upon it after him — without anything being diminished from their rewards. And whoever establishes an evil practice, upon him shall be its burden — and the burden of whoever acts upon it after him, without anything being diminished from their burdens.' Then he recited this verse: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ (and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ (and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam) — "That is, in a manifest Book, and it is definitive (محكم)." Ibn Abbas mentioned from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام): "I am, by Allah, the Manifest Imam; I distinguish the truth from falsehood — I inherited it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, with its chain to Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far from his father from his grandfather (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a tradition: that he said regarding Ali (عليه السلام): "He is the Imam — in whom Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has figured the knowledge of everything."

I say: These two traditions, were they authentic, would not be part of the [exoteric] commentary (التفسير) at all; rather, their content belongs to the inner depth (بطن) of the Qur'an and its allusions (إشاراته). There is no obstacle to Allah granting a servant who has unified Him and purified his servitude to Him the knowledge of what is in the Manifest Book, and he (عليه السلام) is the master of the monotheists (سيد الموحدين) after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Ayahs

وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ إِذْ جَآءَهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (١٣)
Cite for them the example of the inhabitants of the town when the apostles came to it. (13)
إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱثْنَيْنِ فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَعَزَّزْنَا بِثَالِثٍ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُم مُّرْسَلُونَ (١٤)
When We sent to them two [apostles], they impugned both of them. Then We reinforced them with a third, and they said, ‘We have indeed been sent to you.’ (14)
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ (١٥)
They said, ‘You are no other than human beings like us, and the All-beneficent has not sent down anything, and you are only lying.’ (15)
قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا يَعْلَمُ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ (١٦)
They said, ‘Our Lord knows that we have indeed been sent to you, (16)
وَمَا عَلَيْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٧)
and our duty is only to communicate in clear terms.’ (17)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنَا بِكُمْ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهُوا۟ لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٨)
They said, ‘Indeed we take you for a bad omen. If you do not desist we will stone you, and surely a painful punishment will visit you from us.’ (18)
قَالُوا۟ طَـٰٓئِرُكُم مَّعَكُمْ ۚ أَئِن ذُكِّرْتُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ (١٩)
They said, ‘Your bad omens attend you. What! If you are admonished.... Indeed, you are an unrestrained lot.’ (19)
وَجَآءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢٠)
There came a man hurrying from the city outskirts. He said, ‘O my people! Follow the apostles! (20)
ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَن لَّا يَسْـَٔلُكُمْ أَجْرًا وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٢١)
Follow them who do not ask you any reward and they are rightly guided. (21)
وَمَا لِىَ لَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٢)
Why should I not worship Him who has originated me, and to whom you shall be brought back? (22)
ءَأَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً إِن يُرِدْنِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ بِضُرٍّ لَّا تُغْنِ عَنِّى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُنقِذُونِ (٢٣)
Shall I take gods besides Him? If the All-beneficent desired to cause me any distress, their intercession will not avail me in any way, nor will they rescue me. (23)
إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٤)
Indeed, then I would be in manifest error. (24)
إِنِّىٓ ءَامَنتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَٱسْمَعُونِ (٢٥)
Indeed I have faith in your Lord, so listen to me.’ (25)
قِيلَ ٱدْخُلِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ قَوْمِى يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
He was told, ‘Enter paradise!’ He said, ‘Alas! Had my people only known (26)
بِمَا غَفَرَ لِى رَبِّى وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ (٢٧)
for what my Lord forgave me and made me one of the honoured ones!’ (27)
۞ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِن جُندٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا كُنَّا مُنزِلِينَ (٢٨)
After him We did not send down on his people a host from the sky, nor We would have sent down. (28)
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ خَـٰمِدُونَ (٢٩)
It was but a single Cry, and, behold, they were stilled [like burnt ashes]! (29)
يَـٰحَسْرَةً عَلَى ٱلْعِبَادِ ۚ مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٠)
How regrettable of the servants! There did not come to them any apostle but that they used to deride him. (30)
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٣١)
Have they not regarded how many generations We have destroyed before them who will not come back to them? (31)
وَإِن كُلٌّ لَّمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (٣٢)
And all of them will indeed be presented before Us. (32)

(Commentary)

This is a parable comprising both warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير) that Allah, the Glorified, has struck for the generality of the people. In it, He points toward the divine messengership (الرسالة الإلهية) and what the true call (الدعوة الحقة) entails of forgiveness and a noble reward for those who believe in it, follow the Reminder (الذكر), and fear the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) in the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge. It also points to the painful punishment for those who disbelieve and deny it, such that the word [of punishment] becomes due against them. Furthermore, it contains an indication of His oneness, the Exalted, and the return of all people to Him.

There is no contradiction between His informing that they will not believe whether they are warned or not, and the act of warning them; for in the conveyance of the message (البلاغ) lies the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) and the perfection of either felicity (السعادة) or wretchedness (الشقاوة). The Exalted said: لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ — "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (Al-Anfal: 42). And He said: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً — "We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (Al-Isra: 82).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلاً أَصْحابَ الْقَرْيَةِ إِذْ جاءَهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ — "Cite for them the example of the inhabitants of the town when the apostles came to it" (36:13). The parable (المثل) is a discourse or a story by which a specific objective is illustrated so that it becomes clear to the addressee. Since their story clarifies the aforementioned promise and threat, He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to cite it as a parable for them.

It appears that "parable" (مَثَلاً) is the second object of His saying "cite" (اضْرِبْ), and its first object is His saying "the inhabitants of the town" (أَصْحابَ الْقَرْيَةِ). The meaning is: Cite for them the inhabitants of the town and their condition in this state as a parable. The second object was placed first to avoid a disruptive separation [between related grammatical elements].

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْهِمُ اثْنَيْنِ فَكَذَّبُوهُما فَعَزَّزْنا بِثالِثٍ فَقالُوا إِنَّا إِلَيْكُمْ مُرْسَلُونَ — "When We sent to them two [apostles], they impugned both of them. Then We strengthened them with a third, and they said, ‘Indeed we have been sent to you’" (36:14). Strengthening (التعزيز) is derived from "might" (العزة), meaning power and fortification. His saying "When We sent to them" (إِذْ أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْهِمُ) is a detailed exposition of His saying "when the apostles came to it" (إِذْ جاءَهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ).

The meaning is: Cite for them as a parable the inhabitants of the town at the time when We sent to them two messengers from among Our messengers, but they denied them—meaning the two messengers—so We strengthened them with a third messenger. Then the messengers said: "Indeed, we are sent to you from Allah's side."

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا ما أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا وَما أَنْزَلَ الرَّحْمنُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ — "They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us, and the All-beneficent has not sent down anything. You are only lying’" (36:15). They held the view that a human being cannot attain prophethood (النبوة) or revelation (الوحي). They argued this based on themselves, as they found nothing of that sort within their own souls, so they extended this judgment to the souls of the prophets, based on the principle that the judgment of likes is uniform.

According to this interpretation, the meaning of His saying "and the All-beneficent has not sent down anything" (وَما أَنْزَلَ الرَّحْمنُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ) is: Allah has not sent down any revelation; for if He were to send down anything to a human, we would have attained it within ourselves just as you claim for yourselves. Their referring to Allah, the Glorified, as "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is because they, like other idolaters, acknowledged Allah, the Glorified, and His being characterized by noble attributes (١) such as creation, mercy, and sovereignty. However, they believed that He delegated the matter of governance (التدبير) to the near ones among His creation, such as the noble angels; thus, they are the governing lords (الأرباب المدبرون) and the worshipped deities (الآلهة المعبودون), while Allah, exalted is His Name, is the Lord of lords and the God of gods.

It is also possible that the mention of the name "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is in the narration [by Allah] rather than the quoted speech [of the people]. In that case, the expression would be due to His forbearance and mercy, the Exalted, prior to their denial and rejection of the manifest truth.

His saying "You are only lying" (إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ) is in the position of a conclusion to the beginning of the verse. The sum of their argument is: You are humans like us, and we, in our humanity, do not find within our souls anything of the descending revelation that you claim; and since you are like us, the All-Beneficent has not sent down any revelation, so your claim is false. Since you have nothing but this claim, you are doing nothing but lying.

From what has preceded, the subtle point of the restriction (الحصر) in His saying "You are only lying" (إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ) becomes apparent, as does the reason for using the verbal form [lying] rather than saying "you are liars" (كاذبون); for the intent is to negate the act in the present moment rather than implying continuity or the future.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا رَبُّنا يَعْلَمُ إِنَّا إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ وَما عَلَيْنا إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ — "They said, ‘Our Lord knows that we have indeed been sent to you, and our duty is only to communicate in clear terms’" (36:16-17). Allah, the Glorified, did not narrate from these messengers a direct response to the argument of their people: "You are nothing but humans like us," etc.

(١) However, they differ in interpreting them. The Sabians interpret them via negation; thus, the meaning of "the All-Knowing" and "the All-Powerful" according to them is "one who is not ignorant or incapable."

This is similar to what was narrated from the messengers sent to past nations when their nations argued with the same proof: "You are nothing but humans like us." Their messengers responded by saying: إِنْ نَحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَمُنُّ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ — "We are indeed [nothing] but humans like you, but Allah favors whomever He wishes of His servants" (Ibrahim: 11), the explanation of which has already passed.

Rather, He narrated from them that they mentioned to the people that they were sent to them and commanded to convey the message, and that they had no duty beyond that. They were in no need of the people's confirmation or belief; it sufficed them that their Lord knew they were messengers, and they required nothing more than that.

Thus, His saying: قالُوا رَبُّنا يَعْلَمُ إِنَّا إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ is an announcement of their messengership, and the speech was emphasized with the particle inna (إِنَّ), the [prefix] lam (اللام), and the testimony of their Lord's knowledge of that. His saying "Our Lord knows" (رَبُّنا يَعْلَمُ) is a parenthetical phrase functioning as an oath. The meaning is: We are messengers to you, truthful in our claim of messengership, and the knowledge of our Lord who sent us with it is sufficient for us. we have no need for your confirmation, nor do we seek any benefit from you such as a reward; we are not concerned with obtaining such from you. Rather, what concerns us is the conveyance of the message and the completion of the proof.

His saying: وَما عَلَيْنا إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ — Conveyance (البلاغ) is the act of delivering the message. The meaning is: We have not been commanded nor tasked with anything except the conveyance of the message and the completion of the proof.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنا بِكُمْ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهُوا لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُمْ مِنَّا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "They said, ‘Indeed we take you for a bad omen. If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and a painful punishment from us will surely befall you’" (36:18). The speakers are the inhabitants of the town, and the addressees are the messengers. Taking an omen (التطير) is to perceive something as a bad sign (التشؤم). Their saying "If you do not desist" etc., is a threat from them to the messengers.

The meaning is: The inhabitants of the town said to their messengers: We see you as a bad omen, and we swear that if you do not desist from this conveyance and cease this call, we will surely stone you with rocks, and a painful punishment from us will certainly reach and afflict you.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا طائِرُكُمْ مَعَكُمْ أَإِنْ ذُكِّرْتُمْ بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ — "They said, ‘Your omen is with yourselves. What! If you are admonished? Rather you are a profligate lot’" (36:19). The speakers are the messengers addressing the inhabitants of the town.

Regarding His saying: طائِرُكُمْ مَعَكُمْ, the omen (الطائر) originally referred to birds (الطير), which were used to seek omens. Later, the usage expanded to include anything perceived as a bad omen. Sometimes it is used for the events that confront a person, and sometimes for an ill fate—an imaginary concept they view as the source of human misery and deprivation of all good.

In any case, the apparent meaning of His saying "Your omen is with you" is that the thing you ought to take as a bad omen is with you: it is your state of turning away from the Truth, which is monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and your turning toward falsehood, which is polytheism (الشرك).

It is also said the meaning is: Your omen—meaning your fortune and share of good and evil—is with you from your own deeds; if good, then good, and if evil, then evil. This takes "omen" in the second sense, but His subsequent saying "What! If you are admonished? Rather you are a profligate lot" is more appropriate for the first meaning.

His saying: أَإِنْ ذُكِّرْتُمْ is a reproachful interrogation. What is meant by "admonishment" (التذكير) is reminding them of the Truth regarding His oneness, the Exalted, and the return of all to Him, and similar matters. The apodosis of the conditional statement is omitted in the speech, hinting that it is something that should not even be mentioned or spoken. The implied meaning is: "What! If you are reminded of the Truth, do you meet it with such hideous rejection and foul conduct as taking bad omens and making threats?"

His saying: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ means: Rather, you are those who exceed the bounds (مجاوزون للحد) in sin. This is a transition (إضراب) from what preceded. The meaning is: Rather, the primary reason for your rejection and denial of the Truth is that you are a people who persist in profligacy (الإسراف) and exceeding the bounds.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا الْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعى قالَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُوا الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "There came a man running from the city’s edge. He said, ‘O my people! Follow the apostles!’" (36:20). The "city's edge" (أقصى المدينة) is its furthest point relative to a presumed starting point. The "town" (القرية) mentioned at the beginning of the discourse is replaced here with "city" (مدينة) to indicate its greatness. Running (السعي) is to walk quickly.

A similar expression occurs in the story of Moses and the Copt, where it says: وَجاءَ رَجُلٌ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعى — "There came a man running from the city’s edge" (28:20). There, "a man" was placed first, while here it is placed later. Perhaps the subtle point is that the emphasis there was on the arrival of the man and his informing Moses of the council's plot to kill him; thus, "the man" was prioritized, then his personal concern for delivering the news was indicated by "running" (يَسْعى) as a subsequent circumstantial clause. In contrast, the emphasis here is on his coming from the furthest part of the city to show that there was no collusion between him and the messengers regarding the call; thus, "from the city's edge" (مِنْ أَقْصَا الْمَدِينَةِ) was prioritized, while the man and his running were placed later.

There has been intense disagreement among [commentators] regarding the man's name, his father's name, his craft, and his occupation. We are not concerned with engaging in that to understand the intended meaning; had the understanding depended on it even slightly, the Glorified would have indicated it in His speech and not omitted it.

What is important is to contemplate his share of faith in this situation where he rose to support the messengers (peace be upon them) and aid them. According to what contemplation of the narrated speech yields, he was a man whose heart Allah, the Glorified, had illuminated with the light of faith. He believed in Allah with sincere faith, worshipping Him not out of desire for Paradise or fear of Hell, but because He is worthy of worship. For this reason, he was among the honored (المكرمين). Allah, the Glorified, has not described anyone in His speech with this attribute [in an absolute sense] except His near angels and His sincere servants. He debated the people and overcame them, nullifying the arguments they clung to regarding the impermissibility of worshipping Allah alone and the necessity of worshipping their deities. He established the necessity of worshipping Him alone, confirmed the messengers in their claim of messengership, and then believed in them.

His saying, the Exalted: اتَّبِعُوا مَنْ لا يَسْئَلُكُمْ أَجْراً وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — "Follow those who do not ask you for any reward, and who are [rightly] guided" (36:21). This is an exposition of his saying: "Follow the apostles." Placing the phrase "those who do not ask you for any reward, and who are [rightly] guided" in this verse in place of "the apostles" in the previous verse suggests causality (العلية). Its explanation is that the impermissibility of following a speaker in his speech is due to one of two things: either because his speech is misguidance and the speaker is misguided—and it is not permissible to follow the misguided in his misguidance—or because the speech, though true (and the truth must be followed), is spoken by one with a corrupt motive who wishes to use the word of truth as a means to an end, such as acquiring wealth or gaining status and position. However, if the speech is true and the speaker is free from corrupt motives, purified from trickery, deception, and betrayal, then it is obligatory to follow him in his speech. These messengers are guided in their saying "Worship none but Allah," and they do not seek from you any reward of wealth or status; therefore, it is obligatory for you to follow them in their speech.

As for their being guided, it is because of the established proof for the truth of what they call toward—monotheism—and its being the Truth. The proof is his saying: "Why should I not worship..." until the end of the two verses.

As for their not seeking a reward from you, it is indicated by their saying: "Our Lord knows that we have indeed been sent to you," as previously explained.

This explanation supports what we previously stated: that their saying "Our Lord knows that we have indeed been sent to you" was intended to negate any desire for reward or anything else from the people.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما لِيَ لا أَعْبُدُ الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ أَأَتَّخِذُ مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً — until His saying — وَلا يُنْقِذُونِ — "Why should I not worship Him who originated me, and to whom you shall be brought back? Shall I take gods besides Him?..." (36:22-23). He began to exhaust the proof for monotheism and the negation of [other] deities in two verses.

He chose for this the style of the first-person singular, except for a clause he inserted in the middle of the speech, which is His saying: "and to whom you shall be brought back" (وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ). He did this by applying the judgment to himself as a human being whom Allah brought into existence and originated (فطره), so that it might apply to every human who is like him—for individuals are likes. Thus, his saying "Why should I not worship..." etc., is in the sense of: "Why should a human not worship..." etc., and "Shall a human take gods besides Him?..." etc.

He expressed [the concept of] Allah by saying: "Him who originated me" (الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي) to indicate causality. For Allah's originating the human and bringing him into existence after non-existence necessitates that everything belonging to the human—essence, attributes, and actions—returns to Him, the Exalted, subsists through Him, and is owned by Him. Thus, the human has nothing but pure servitude (العبودية). It is therefore incumbent upon the human to place himself in the station of servitude and manifest it toward Him, the Exalted; and this is worship (العبادة). Thus, he must worship Him, the Exalted, because He is worthy of it.

This is what we pointed to just now: that the man worshipped Allah with sincerity (الإخلاص) to Him, not out of desire for Paradise or fear of Hell, but because He is worthy of worship.

Since faith in Him, the Exalted, and worshipping Him in this manner is something the generality of people do not attain—for most of them worship out of fear, or desire, or both—the man turned to the people after explaining his own state and said: "and to whom you shall be brought back" (وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ). By this, he intended to warn them of the Day of Return, and that He, the Exalted, will call them to account for what they did and recompense them for their evil deeds. Thus, His saying "and to whom you shall be brought back" is like a parenthetical clause departing from the [first-person] style, or it is exactly that.

Furthermore, the two verses are two established proofs for the invalidation of what the polytheists argued, upon which they built the worship of idols and their lords.

To clarify: they said that Allah, the Glorified, is too sublime to be encompassed by sense, imagination, or intellect; nothing of the cognitive faculties can reach Him. Therefore, it is impossible to turn to Him in worship. Thus, the way of worship is to turn to those near to His presence and the powerful among His creation, such as the noble angels, the jinn, and the saintly among humans, so that they may be intercessors for us with Allah in bringing benefits and repelling evils and hardships.

The response to the first of the two arguments is, in essence, that although man does not encompass the Transcendent Essence in knowledge, he knows Him, the Exalted, through His specific attributes, such as His being his Originator and the one who brought him into existence. Thus, he can turn to Him through these attributes, and to deny the possibility of this is mere obstinacy. This response is what He indicated by His saying: "Why should I not worship Him who originated me."

The response to the second argument is that if these deities have any intercession (شفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — it is from what Allah has bestowed upon them. Allah, the Glorified, does not grant them that except in matters where His definitive will is not concerned. It follows that their intercession is only in what Allah has permitted for them, as He said: ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ — "there is no intercessor, except after His leave" (Yunus: 3). However, if Allah wills something with a definitive will, their intercession avails nothing in preventing its execution. Thus, taking them as gods or not is the same in terms of their lack of influence in bringing good or repelling evil. He indicated this by His saying: "Shall I take gods besides Him? If the All-beneficent should desire any adversity for me, their intercession will not avail me anything, nor will they rescue me."

His expressing [the concept of] Allah as "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is an indication of the vastness and abundance of His mercy, and that all blessings are from Him, and the governance of good and evil belongs to Him. From here, another proof is derived for His oneness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية). Since all blessings, as well as the order prevailing within them, are from His mercy and subsist through Him without any independence in any part of them, the one who is independent in governance is He, the Exalted. Even the governance of the angels—if their governance of anything were assumed—is from His mercy and His governance, the Exalted. Thus, Lordship belongs to Him, the Exalted, alone, as does Divinity (الألوهية).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي إِذاً لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — "I would then be in manifest error" (36:24) is a formal declaration of the misguidance of taking [other] gods.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي آمَنْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَاسْمَعُونِ — "Indeed I have believed in your Lord, so listen to me" (36:25) is from the speech of the man addressing the messengers. His saying "so listen to me" (فاسمعون) is a metaphor for bearing witness to his testimony. His saying "Indeed I have believed in your Lord" etc., is a renewal of the testimony to the Truth and a confirmation of faith. The apparent context is that he only said "Indeed I have believed in your Lord" after his debate, addressing the messengers to have them bear witness to his faith and to support them with his belief in the sight and hearing of the people.

It is also said that it is an address to the people in support of the messengers, meaning: "I have believed in Allah, so listen to me, for I do not care what comes from you because of that." Or the meaning is: "I have believed in Allah, so listen to me and believe in Him." Or he intended by it to provoke them and distract them from the messengers toward himself, as he saw they were intent on harming them.

However, this does not harmonize with expressing [the concept of] Allah by saying "your Lord" (بِرَبِّكُمْ), for the people did not take Him, the Exalted, as their Lord; they only worshipped lords besides Allah, the Glorified.

This has been countered by saying the meaning is: "I have believed in your Lord for whose lordship over you the proof has been established, and He is Allah, the Glorified." But this is a restriction without a restrictor.

His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ ادْخُلِ الْجَنَّةَ قالَ يا لَيْتَ قَوْمِي يَعْلَمُونَ بِما غَفَرَ لِي رَبِّي وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ — "He was told, ‘Enter paradise!’ He said, ‘Alas! If only my people knew how my Lord has forgiven me and made me one of the honored!’" (36:26-27). The address is to the man, and it—as the context suggests—hints that the people killed him. Thus, he was called from the Presence of Might: "Enter Paradise!" This is supported by His subsequent saying: "We did not send down on his people after him..." etc. Placing His saying "He was told, ‘Enter paradise!’" in the position of informing about their killing him indicates that there was no gap or separation between his being killed at their hands and his being commanded to enter Paradise; it is as if his being killed at their hands was his command to enter Paradise.

The "Paradise" intended here is the paradise of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — rather than the Paradise of the Hereafter. The claim of some that it refers to the Paradise of the Hereafter—meaning he will be told "Enter Paradise" on the Day of Resurrection, with the past tense used to indicate certainty of occurrence—is an arbitrary assertion without proof. Similarly, it is said that Allah raised him to heaven and he was told "Enter Paradise," so he is alive and enjoying bliss therein until the Hour begins; this is also an arbitrary assertion like the one before it.

It is also said that the ones who said "Enter Paradise" were the people, saying it to him when they killed him in mockery. However, this does not harmonize with what Allah, the Glorified, narrated from him afterward: "He said, ‘Alas! If only my people knew...’" etc. For the apparent meaning is that he wished his people knew the state he was in after hearing the call "Enter Paradise," and nothing preceded in the speech upon which such a statement [by the people] could be based.

His saying: قالَ يا لَيْتَ قَوْمِي يَعْلَمُونَ بِما غَفَرَ لِي رَبِّي وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ is a new sentence like the one before it, functioning as a response to a presumed question, as if it were asked: "What happened after his support for the messengers?" So it was said: "He was told, ‘Enter paradise!’" Then it was asked: "What happened after that?" So it was said: "He said, ‘Alas! If only my people knew...’" etc. This was sincere advice from him to his people while dead, just as he used to advise them while alive.

The particle ma (ما) in His saying "for how [my Lord] has forgiven me" (بِما غَفَرَ لِي) is a gerundive particle (مصدرية). His saying "and made me" (وَجَعَلَنِي) is conjoined to "forgave" (غَفَرَ). The meaning is: "for my Lord's forgiveness of me and His making me one of the honored."

The gift of "honoring" (الإكرام), although broad and attained by many—such as being honored with blessings as in His saying: فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ — "As for man, when his Lord tests him and honors him and blesses him, he says, ‘My Lord has honored me’" (89:15), and His saying: إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ — "Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13), for the servant's nobility with Allah is an honoring from Him to him—nevertheless, He has not counted as "the honored" (المكرمين) in an absolute sense except two groups of His creation: the noble angels, as in His saying: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — "Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command" (21:26-27); and those complete in their faith among the believers, whether they are from the sincere (المخلصين) — those who purify their religion for Allah — as in His saying: أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ — "They will be in gardens, [highly] honored" (70:35), or from the chosen (المخلصين) — those whom Allah has purified for Himself — as in His saying: إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — until He said — وَهُمْ مُكْرَمُونَ — "except the chosen servants of Allah... and they will be [highly] honored" (37:40-42).

The verse is among the proofs for the existence of the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَنْزَلَنا عَلى قَوْمِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ مِنْ جُنْدٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَما كُنَّا مُنْزِلِينَ — "After him We did not send down any host from the sky on his people, nor do We ever send down" (36:28). The two pronouns refer to the man. "After him" (مِنْ بَعْدِهِ) means after his killing. The first and third occurrences of "from" (مِنْ) indicate the starting point, while the second is an augmentative particle to emphasize the negation.

The verse is a preamble to the following verse, and it is intended to show the insignificance of the people's matter and the ease of taking vengeance upon them by destroying them for Allah, the Glorified. He does not need, in destroying them, any equipment or numbers such that He would send down from heaven a host of angels to fight them and destroy them. He did not do that to them, nor did He do that in destroying those He destroyed of the past nations; He only destroyed them with a single cry that finished them off.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ كانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً ، فَإِذا هُمْ خامِدُونَ — "It was but a single cry, and behold, they were stilled" (36:29). That is, the matter that was the cause of their destruction by Our will was nothing but a single cry. The verb is feminine because the predicate is feminine. The indefiniteness of "cry" (صَيْحَةً) and its description as "single" (واحدة) are for belittlement. Stilling (الخمود) is silence and lack of motion. The sentence is a new start, functioning as a response to a presumed question, as if it were asked: "What was the cause of their destruction?" So it was said: "It was but a single cry."

The meaning is: The cause of their destruction was the simplest of matters, which was a single cry. Then, suddenly, silence overcame them, and they became still, with no sound heard from them; they were dead to the last man, motionless.

His saying, the Exalted: يا حَسْرَةً عَلَى الْعِبادِ ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — "Alas for the servants! No apostle ever came to them but they used to deride him" (36:30). That is: O the regret of the servants! The calling upon "regret" (الحسرة) for them is more eloquent than simply attributing it to them. The cause of the regret is what is contained in His saying: "No apostle ever came to them..." etc.

From this context, it is understood that "the servants" (العباد) refers to the generality of people. The regret is intensified by their being "servants," for a servant's rejection of his master's call and his rebellion against him is more hideous than anyone else's rejection of a counselor's advice.

By this, the weakness of the view of those who said that "the servants" refers to the messengers, or the angels, or both together, becomes apparent. Likewise, the view of those who said that "the servants" refers to the people, but the one expressing regret is the man.

It also becomes clear that His saying "Alas for the servants!" etc., is from the speech of Allah, the Exalted, and not a continuation of the man's speech.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ — "Have they not regarded how many generations We have destroyed before them, who will not come back to them?" (36:31). This is a reproach to those for whom the regret was called. "Of the generations" (مِنَ الْقُرُونِ) is an exposition of "how many" (كَمْ). Generations (القرون) is the plural of qarn, which refers to the people of a single era.

His saying "who will not come back to them" (أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ) is an exposition of His saying "how many generations We have destroyed before them." The first plural pronoun refers to the generations, while the second and third refer to the servants.

The meaning is: Have they not taken a lesson from the great number of those destroyed by Allah's command from the past generations, and that they are seized by a divine seizing such that they are unable to return to the luxury they were in?

The commentators have other views regarding the referents of the pronouns and the meaning of the verse that are far from [sound] understanding, so we have omitted mentioning them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كُلٌّ لَمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنا مُحْضَرُونَ — "And all of them will surely be brought before Us" (36:32). The word in (إِنْ) is a particle of negation, and "all" (كُلٌ) is a subject whose nunation compensates for the omitted genitive. Lamma (لَمَّا) is in the sense of "except" (إلا). "All together" (جميع) means gathered. "Before Us" (لدينا) is an adverbial phrase related to it. "Brought" (محضَرون) is a second predicate after "all together," though some considered it an adjective for "all together."

The meaning is: None of them are anything but gathered before Us, brought for accounting and recompense on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ — "That is a day on which mankind will be gathered, and that is a day witnessed [by all]" (Hud: 103).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': They said: Jesus sent two messengers from the disciples to the city of Antioch. When they drew near the city, they saw an old man tending some small sheep of his—he was Habib, the companion of Yasin. They greeted him, and the old man said to them: "Who are you?" They said: "Messengers of Jesus. We call you from the worship of idols to the worship of the All-Beneficent." He said: "Do you have a sign?" They said: "Yes, we heal the sick and cure the blind and the leper by the permission of Allah, the Exalted."

The old man said: "I have a son who has been sick and bedridden for years." They said: "Then take us to your house that we may look upon his condition." He went with them, and they wiped his son, and he immediately stood up healthy by the permission of Allah, the Exalted. The news spread in the city, and Allah healed many sick people at their hands.

They had a king who worshipped idols. The news reached him, so he summoned them and said to them: "Who are you?" They said: "Messengers of Jesus. We have come to call you from the worship of that which neither hears nor sees to the worship of Him who hears and sees." The king said: "Do we have a god other than our gods?" They said: "Yes, the One who brought you and your gods into existence." He said: "Stand aside until I consider your matter." Then the people seized them in the market and beat them.

Wahb ibn Munabbih said: Jesus sent these two messengers to Antioch. They arrived but did not reach its king, and the duration of their stay grew long. The king went out one day, and they proclaimed the greatness of Allah and mentioned Him. The king became angry and ordered them to be imprisoned, and each of them was given a hundred lashes.

When the two messengers were denied and beaten, Jesus sent Simon Peter (شمعون الصفا), the head of the disciples, regarding their matter to support them. Simon entered the city in disguise and began to associate with the king's entourage until they became familiar with him. They reported news of him to the king, who summoned him and was pleased with his company, becoming fond of him and honoring him. Then Simon said to him one day: "O king, it has reached me that you have imprisoned two men in the dungeon and beaten them when they called you to other than your religion. Have you heard their speech?" The king said: "Anger came between me and that." He said: "If the king sees fit, let him summon them so we may see what they have."

The king summoned them, and Simon said to them: "Who sent you here?" They said: "Allah, who created everything, having no partner." He said: "And what sign has He given you?" They said: "Whatever you desire." The king ordered that a boy with sightless eyes be brought, whose eye sockets were like his forehead. They did not cease praying to Allah until the place of sight split open. They took two pellets of clay and placed them in the sockets, and they became two eyeballs with which he could see. The king was amazed. Then Simon said to the king: "What if you were to ask your god to perform a deed like this? It would be an honor for you and your people." The king said: "I have no secret from you; our god whom we worship neither harms nor benefits."

Then the king said to the two messengers: "If your God is able to revive a dead person, we will believe in Him and in you." They said: "Our God is powerful over all things." The king said: "There is a dead person here who died seven days ago; we have not buried him until his father returns, who was away." They brought the dead person, who had changed and begun to smell. They began to pray to their Lord openly, and Simon began to pray to his Lord secretly. Then the dead man stood up and said to them: "I died seven days ago and was entered into seven valleys of fire, and I warn you of the state you are in, so believe in Allah." The king was amazed. When Simon knew that his words had affected the king, he called him to Allah, and he believed, as did a group of the people of his kingdom, while others disbelieved.

He said: Al-Ayyashi narrated similar to that with his chain from al-Thumali and others from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them), except that in some narrations: Allah sent the two messengers to the people of Antioch, then sent the third. In some of them: Allah revealed to Jesus to send them, then he sent his legatee (وصيه) Simon to rescue them; and that the dead person whom Allah revived through their prayer was the king's son, and that he came out of his grave shaking the dust from his head. He said to him: "O my son, what is your condition?" He said: "I was dead, and I saw two men prostrating, asking Allah, the Exalted, to revive me." He said: "O my son, would you recognize them if you saw them?" He said: "Yes." So he brought the people out to the desert, and man after man passed by him. One of them passed after a large crowd, and he said: "This is one of them." Then the other passed, and he recognized them and pointed with his hand toward them. So the king and the people of his kingdom believed.

Ibn Ishaq said: Rather, the king disbelieved, and he and his people resolved to kill the messengers. News of this reached Habib while he was at the furthest gate of the city, so he came running to them, reminding them and calling them to obey the messengers.

I say: The context of the verses of the story does not harmonize with some of these narrations.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu Dawud, Abu Nu'aym, Ibn Asakir, and al-Daylami narrated from Abu Layla who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "The truthful ones (الصديقين) are three: Habib the Carpenter, the believer of the people of Yasin, who said: يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُوا الْمُرْسَلِينَ (O my people! Follow the apostles!); Ezekiel, the believer of the people of Pharaoh, who said: أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلاً أَنْ يَقُولَ رَبِّيَ اللهُ (Do you kill a man for saying, ‘My Lord is Allah’?); and Ali ibn Abi Talib, and he is the best of them."

I say: It was also narrated from al-Bukhari in his Tarikh from Ibn Abbas from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its wording is: "The truthful ones are three: Ezekiel, the believer of the people of Pharaoh; Habib the Carpenter, the companion of the people of Yasin; and Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In al-Majma', from the Tafsir of al-Tha'labi with the chain from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Layla from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله who said: "The foremost of the nations (سباق الأمم) are three who did not disbelieve in Allah for the blink of an eye: Ali ibn Abi Talib, the companion of Yasin, and the believer of the people of Pharaoh. They are the truthful ones, and Ali is the best of them."

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh—and he weakened it—from Ibn Abbas from him عليه‌السلام, and its wording is: "The foremost (السبق) are three: the foremost to Moses was Joshua ibn Nun; the foremost to Jesus was the companion of Yasin; and the foremost to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was Ali ibn Abi Talib."

Ayahs

وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْنَـٰهَا وَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهَا حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ (٣٣)
A sign for them is the dead earth, which We revive and bring forth grain out of it, so they eat of it. (33)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ وَفَجَّرْنَا فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْعُيُونِ (٣٤)
We make in it orchards of date palms and vines, and We cause springs to gush forth in it, (34)
لِيَأْكُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦ وَمَا عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٥)
so that they may eat of its fruit and what their hands have cultivated. Will they not then give thanks? (35)
سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٦)
Immaculate is He who has created all the kinds of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know. (36)
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فَإِذَا هُم مُّظْلِمُونَ (٣٧)
A sign for them is the night, which We strip of daylight, and, behold, they find themselves in the dark! (37)
وَٱلشَّمْسُ تَجْرِى لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَّهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٣٨)
The sun runs on to its place of rest: That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (38)
وَٱلْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْنَـٰهُ مَنَازِلَ حَتَّىٰ عَادَ كَٱلْعُرْجُونِ ٱلْقَدِيمِ (٣٩)
As for the moon, We have ordained its phases, until it becomes like an old palm leaf. (39)
لَا ٱلشَّمْسُ يَنۢبَغِى لَهَآ أَن تُدْرِكَ ٱلْقَمَرَ وَلَا ٱلَّيْلُ سَابِقُ ٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ (٤٠)
Neither it behooves the sun to overtake the moon, nor may the night outrun the day, and each swims in an orbit. (40)
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (٤١)
A sign for them is that We carried their progeny in the laden ship, (41)
وَخَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ مَا يَرْكَبُونَ (٤٢)
and We have created for them what is similar to it, which they ride. (42)
وَإِن نَّشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلَا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنقَذُونَ (٤٣)
And if We like We drown them, whereat they have no one to call for help, nor are they rescued (43)
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٤٤)
except by a mercy from Us and for an enjoyment until some time. (44)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَمَا خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٤٥)
And when they are told, ‘Beware of that which is before you and that which is behind you, so that you may receive [His] mercy…’ (45)
وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٤٦)
There does not come to them any sign from among the signs of their Lord but that they have been disregarding it. (46)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنُطْعِمُ مَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُۥٓ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٧)
When they are told, ‘Spend out of what Allah has provided you,’ the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Shall we feed [someone] whom Allah would feed, if He wished? You are only in manifest error.’ (47)

(Commentary)

After relating to them the story of the People of the Town and the fate that befell them due to their polytheism (الشرك) and their denial of the messengers, and after rebuking them for belittling the matter of the message and warning them of the descent of punishment upon them—just as it descended upon the deniers of former generations—and informing them that they shall all be brought forth for reckoning and recompense;

He now presents signs (آيات) from creation and governance (التدبير) that point to His Lordship (الربوبية) and His Divinity (الألوهية), the Exalted, alone without any partner. Then He rebukes them for failing to reflect upon the signs of Oneness (الوحدانية) and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the final destination of souls — and for turning away from them, mocking the Truth, and withholding spending (الإنفاق) for the poor and the destitute.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ الْأَرْضُ الْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْناها وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ — A sign for them is the dead earth: We revive it and bring forth from it grain, so they eat of it. (36:33). In this verse and the two following it, the Glorified mentions a sign from the signs of Lordship (الربوبية), which is the governance of the matter of people's sustenance and their nourishment from the fruits of plants, including grains, dates, grapes, and others.

Thus, His saying: وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ الْأَرْضُ الْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْناها — A sign for them is the dead earth: We revive it — although its apparent meaning is that the sign is the earth itself, these two clauses serve as a preamble to His saying: وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا — and bring forth from it grain — etc. They are directed toward indicating that these plant-based foods are among the effects of breathing life into the dead earth and transforming it into grain and fruit from which they eat. Therefore, the sign, upon reflection, is the dead earth in terms of the manifestation of these properties within it and the completion of the governance of people's sustenance through it.
