His saying: وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا — and bring forth from it grain — means: We brought forth from the earth, by causing plants to grow, grain such as wheat, barley, rice, and other legumes.

His saying: فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ — so they eat of it — is a consequence of bringing forth the grain, for it is through eating that the governance is completed. The pronoun in فَمِنْهُ (of it) refers to the grain.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا فِيها جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَأَعْنابٍ وَفَجَّرْنا فِيها مِنَ الْعُيُونِ — We have made in it orchards of palms and vines, and We have caused springs to gush forth in it, (36:34). Al-Raghib said: "The garden (الجنة) is every orchard possessing trees that cover the ground with their foliage." End quote. Date-palms (النخيل) is the plural of nakhl, which is well-known. Grapes (الأعناب) is the plural of inab, a term applied both to the tree—which is the vine—and to the fruit.

Al-Raghib said: "The eye (العين) is the organ of sight..." until he said: "The term ayn is used metaphorically for various meanings found in the organ of sight from different perspectives..." until he said: "And the source of water is called an ayn (spring) by way of comparison to it, because of the water it contains." End quote. Gushing (التفجير) in the earth is the cleaving of it to bring forth water. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَأْكُلُوا مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ — that they may eat of its fruits and what their hands have not wrought. Will they not then be grateful? (36:35). The lam (لـ) is to explain the cause of what was mentioned in the previous verse; that is: We made gardens therein and caused springs to gush forth by cleaving the earth so that people may eat of its fruit.

Regarding His saying: مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ — of its fruit — it has been said that the pronoun refers to that which was "made" (المجعول) of the gardens, which is why it is singular and masculine, rather than saying min thamariha (of their fruit, i.e., the gardens) or min thamarihima (of the fruit of both, i.e., the palms and vines).

It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the mentioned subject, and sometimes a pronoun functions like a demonstrative pronoun (اسم الإشارة), as in the poem of Ru'bah: In it are lines of black and white, As if it (ka-annahu) were the variegation of vitiligo on the skin. It is narrated that Abu Ubaydah asked him about his saying ka-annahu (as if it), and he replied: "It was that (dhalika)."

There are other poor opinions regarding the reference of the pronoun in مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ (of its fruit), such as the view of some that the pronoun refers only to the date-palms; or the view that it refers to the water because the "springs" indicate it, or by assuming a suppressed possessive (مضاف) where the intended meaning is "the water of the springs"; or the view that the pronoun refers to the "gushing" (التفجير) understood from فَجَّرْنا (We caused to gush), with "fruit" in these two views meaning "benefit"; or the view that the pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, and its attribution to Him is because He created it and owns it.

Regarding His saying: وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ — and what their hands have not wrought — work (العمل) is action (الفعل). The difference between them—according to what Al-Raghib mentioned—is that "work" is most often used for an action accompanied by intent and will. For this reason, its use for animals and inanimate objects is rare. Also for this reason, "work" is described as righteous (الصلاح) or otherwise; thus one says "a righteous deed" (عمل صالح) or "an evil deed" (عمل طالح), whereas absolute "action" is not so described.

The ma (ما) in وَما عَمِلَتْهُ is negating (نافية), and the meaning is: "And their hands did not produce the fruit such that they would be partners with Us in the governance (تدبير) of sustenance; rather, it is among that which We have uniquely created and through which We completed the governance without seeking their assistance. So why is it that they do not give thanks?"

This meaning is supported by His saying at the end of the Surah, where He reminds them of His favor in creating livestock for the governance of their sustenance and life: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا أَنْعاماً — Have they not seen that We have created for them cattle out of the handiwork of Our hands, — until He says — وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ وَلَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ وَمَشارِبُ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ — and of them they eat. There are [other] profits for them in them and drinks. Will they not then be grateful? (36:71-73).

Some have considered the possibility that the ma (ما) in وَما عَمِلَتْهُ is a relative pronoun (موصولة) conjoined to "its fruit" (ثمره). The meaning would then be: "That they may eat of its fruit and of that which their hands have wrought from its fruit, such as vinegar and syrup extracted from dates, grapes, and so on."

This view, although some consider it more plausible than the previous one, is not quite correct. This is because the context is one of explaining signs (آيات) that point to His Lordship, the Exalted, by mentioning matters of governance (التدبير) that belong exclusively to Him. It is not suitable to mention any part of another's governance alongside His when establishing the proof. If the intent were to mention their work insofar as it traces back to His creation and is part of the general governance, it would have been more appropriate to say: "And what We guided them to work," or something conveying that meaning, to negate any illusion of partnership in governance.

Others have suggested that ma (ما) is an indefinite noun described by a clause (نكرة موصوفة) conjoined to "its fruit." The meaning would be: "That they may eat of its fruit and of something their hands have wrought." The same objection raised against the previous view applies here as well.

His saying: أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ — Will they not then be grateful? — is a rebuke and a denunciation of their lack of gratitude. Their gratitude to Him, the Exalted, for this governance consists of manifesting the beauty of His blessings by mentioning Him in word and deed—that is, by manifesting that they are His servants, governed by His governance, which is worship (العبادة). Thus, gratitude to Him, the Exalted, is the recognition of His Lordship and taking Him as the God to be worshipped.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لا يَعْلَمُونَ — Immaculate is He who has created all the pairs, of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know. (36:36). This is an instantiation of His glorification (تنزيهه), the Exalted. Since He mentioned their lack of gratitude to Him for what He created for them of various plants and the sustenance He provided of grains and fruits—and He only brought that about by pairing some plants with others, as He said: وَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ — and We have caused every delightful kind [of plant] to grow in it (50:7)—He pointed to something even greater and more expansive than the creation of plant pairs. This is the creation of all pairs (الأزواج) and the organization of the witnessed world by the procreation of everything from an active (فاعل) and a passive (منفعل) element before it, which are its two parents, like the male and female among humans, animals, and plants. Every active and passive element that meet produce a third matter by their meeting. He, the Exalted, pointed to this and glorified Himself by saying: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها — Immaculate is He who has created all the pairs — etc. Thus, His saying: سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها is an instantiation of glorification (تسبيح) as dictated by the context, not merely a report.

His saying: مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ — of what the earth grows — and what follows it is an elucidation of the "pairs." That which the earth grows is the plants, and it is not far-fetched that it includes animals as well. Indeed, He, the Exalted, said regarding man—who is a species of animal: وَاللهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً — And Allah made you grow from the earth [like a] plant (71:17). This is supported by the fact that the apparent context of the elucidation (البيان) is to encompass the subject being elucidated (المبين), yet animals were not mentioned in the enumeration of pairs.

His saying: وَمِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ — and of themselves — meaning human beings. And His saying: وَمِمَّا لا يَعْلَمُونَ — and of what they do not know — which refers to that part of creation which man is ignorant of, or ignorant of the manner of its appearance or the appearance of multiplicity within it.

It has sometimes been said regarding the verse that "pairs" (الأزواج) refers to types and categories. However, this is not supported by the verses that mention the creation of pairs, such as His saying: وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ — In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition. (51:49). Pairing and a kind of harmony and composition are among the necessary implications of the concept of "pair-ness" (الزوجية).

Al-Raghib said: "The term zawj (pair/spouse) is applied to each one of a couple, male and female, among mating animals. It is also applied to each of a pair in other things, such as a pair of boots or sandals. It is also applied to everything that is coupled with another, whether similar to it or its opposite." He said: "And His saying: خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ (We created pairs) explains that everything in the world is a pair insofar as it has an opposite, a like, or a composition; indeed, it is never devoid of composition in some way." End quote.

Thus, the "pair-ness" of a pair is its being dependent for its realization upon harmony and composition. Therefore, each of the two companions, insofar as they are companions, is called a zawj (pair) because of its need for its companion. Likewise, the sum of the two companions is called a zawj because of its need for harmony and composition to be realized as a pair. Thus, things being "pairs" means the coupling of some with others to produce a third, or its being generated from the harmony of two.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ اللَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ النَّهارَ فَإِذا هُمْ مُظْلِمُونَ — A sign for them is the night: We strip the day of it, and lo! they are plunged in darkness. (36:37). This is another sign from the signs of Lordship pointing to the occurrence of the general celestial governance (التدبير) for the human world, mentioned across four verses.

There is no doubt that the sign points to the sudden onset of night following the departure of day. Stripping (السلخ) in the verse carries the meaning of "extracting" (الإخراج), which is why it is made transitive with the preposition min (from). If it carried the meaning of "removing" (النزع), as in our saying: "I stripped the hide from (an) the sheep," it would necessarily be transitive with an rather than min.

This is supported by the fact that He, the Exalted, in various places in His speech, describes the arrival of each of the night and day after the other as an "interweaving" (إيلاج) of one into the other. He said in several places: يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ — He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night. (22:61). If the arrival of day after night is an interweaving of day into night conceptually, then the sudden onset of night after day is an extraction of day from night conceptually.

It is as if the night had covered them and its darkness surrounded them, then the day interwoven into it, so its light and radiance encompassed them; then it exited from it, and the night suddenly came upon them again by the closing in of darkness and its surrounding what the day had illuminated. Thus, there is a kind of metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية) in the speech.

Perhaps what we have mentioned of this perspective is sufficient to replace the lengthy discussions they have engaged in regarding the meaning of "stripping the day from the night" and the subsequent sudden onset of night.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ — And the sun runs on to its resting place. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (36:38). Its "running" (جريها) is its motion. His saying لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها — to its resting place — the lam (لـ) carries the meaning of "toward" (إلى) or indicates the end-goal (الغاية). The "resting place" (المستقر) is a mim-infinitve (مصدر ميمي) or a noun of time or place. The meaning is that it moves toward its resting place or until it reaches its resting place—that is, its stability and stillness by the expiration of its term, or the time of its stability, or its location.

As for its "running," which is its motion, the apparent sensory observation establishes for it a circular motion around the earth. However, scientific research dictates the opposite and reveals that it, along with its planets, has a translational motion toward the constellation Lyra (النسر الواقع).

In any case, the sum of the meaning is that the sun continues to run as long as the worldly system remains as it is, until it settles and becomes still by the expiration of its term, whereupon the world will be destroyed and this system will be nullified. This meaning ultimately returns to the meaning of the recitation attributed to the People of the House (أهل البيت) and others: "And the sun runs, having no resting place for it" (لا مستقر لها), as has been said.

As for interpreting its "running" as its rotational motion around its center, this is contrary to the apparent meaning of "running" (الجري), which indicates translation from one place to another.

His saying: ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ — That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing — means that the aforementioned running is an ordaining and a governance from One who is not overcome by any overcomer in His will, nor is He ignorant of the aspects of benefit in His acts.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْناهُ مَنازِلَ حَتَّى عادَ كَالْعُرْجُونِ الْقَدِيمِ — As for the moon, We have ordained mansions for it, until it becomes like an old palm-stalk. (36:39). Mansions (المنازل) is the plural of manzil, a noun of place derived from "descending" (النزول). It is apparent that what is intended are the twenty-eight mansions that the moon traverses in approximately every twenty-eight days and nights.

The palm-stalk (العرجون) is the stem of the date-cluster from the branchlets to its base; it is a yellow, curved stem that resembles the crescent moon. Old (القديم) means ancient.

Views have differed regarding the meaning of the verse due to the difference in its grammatical structure. The closest interpretation to understanding is the view of those who say the estimation is: "And the moon, We have ordained it as possessing mansions," or "We have ordained mansions for it until it returns as a crescent resembling an ancient, yellowed palm-stalk."

The verse points to the varying appearances of the moon relative to the inhabitants of the earth. Its light is acquired from the sun; approximately half of its sphere is illuminated by it, while the other half, not facing the sun, is dark. Then the position of illumination changes and continues to do so until it returns to the first position. This results in it appearing to the inhabitants of the earth in the form of a crescent, then the light continues to spread over it until it becomes a full moon, then it continues to decrease until it returns to what it was at its beginning.

The variation of its forms has prominent effects on land and sea and the life of people, as explained in relevant research.

Thus, the noble verse mentions, as a sign of the moon, its states that occur relative to the earth and its people, rather than its state in itself or its state relative to the sun only.

From here, it is not far-fetched to say regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها — And the sun runs on to its resting place — that the intent of His saying تَجْرِي (runs) is an allusion to what the apparent sense gives of its daily, seasonal, and annual motion, which is its state relative to us; and by His saying لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها (to its resting place), its state in itself is intended, which is its stillness relative to its planets moving around it. It is as if it were said: "And a sign for them is that the sun, despite its stability, runs for them, and the All-mighty, the All-knowing has thereby governed the existence of the terrestrial world and the life of its inhabitants." And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا الشَّمْسُ يَنْبَغِي لَها أَنْ تُدْرِكَ الْقَمَرَ وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ وَكُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ — Neither it behooves the sun to overtake the moon, nor may the night outrun the day, and each swims in an orbit. (36:40). The word yanbaghi (behooves/is possible) indicates preference, and the negation of the preference of overtaking for the sun is a negation of its occurrence from it. What is intended is that the governance is not something that runs one day and stops another; rather, it is a permanent governance, neither disordered nor nullified, until the term appointed by Him, the Exalted, for that purpose expires.

The meaning is that the sun and the moon are constant in the course mapped out for them; thus, the sun does not overtake the moon such that the governance carried out by them would be disordered. Nor does the night outrun the day, for they alternate in governance; it does not happen that the night precedes the day such that two nights join together, then two days, but rather they alternate.

He did not mention the negation of the moon overtaking the sun, nor the negation of the day outrunning the night, because the context is one of explaining the preservation of the divine order from disorder and corruption. Thus, He negated the overtaking of that which is greater and stronger—the sun—of that which is smaller and weaker—the moon; from this, the reverse case is understood. He also negated the outrunning of the night, which is the absence of the day that follows it, for the night is naturally subsequent to it; from this, the reverse case is also understood.

His saying: وَكُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ — and each swims in an orbit — means that each of the sun, the moon, and others among the stars and planets runs in a course specific to it, just as a fish swims in water. Thus, the orbit (الفلك) is the spatial path in which the celestial body moves. It is not far-fetched then that "each" (كل) refers to each of the sun, the moon, the night, and the day, even if there is nothing in His speech, the Exalted, to testify to that.

The use of the plural pronoun specific to rational beings in His saying يَسْبَحُونَ (they swim) is perhaps to indicate their compliance with His will and their obedience to His command, the Exalted, like rational beings, as in His saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى إِلَى السَّماءِ وَهِيَ دُخانٌ فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ — Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly.’ (41:11).

The commentators have other unsettled opinions regarding the sentences of this verse, which we have omitted; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the detailed works.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآيَةٌ لَهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ — A sign for them is that We carried their progeny in the laden ship, (36:41). Al-Raghib said: "Progeny (الذرية) originally refers to small children; in common usage, it applies to both small and large together. It is used for the singular and the plural, though its origin is for the plural." End quote. The ship (الفلك) is the vessel, and laden (المشحون) means filled.

This is another sign from the signs of His Lordship, the Exalted, which is the operation of His governance in the sea, where He carries their progeny in the ship laden with them and their goods, crossing with it from one side to another for trade and other purposes. There is no carrier for them therein, nor any protector for them from drowning, except Him, the Exalted. The properties they benefit from in seafaring are matters subjected to Him, the Exalted, tracing back to His creation; moreover, these causes, if they did not trace back to Him, the Exalted, would be of no avail.

The carrying was attributed to the "progeny" rather than themselves—He did not say "We carried them"—to stir compassion and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: وَخَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ — and We have created for them the like of it which they ride. (36:42). What is intended by it—according to what they have interpreted—is livestock. He, the Exalted, said: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ — and He has made for you ships and cattle that you ride, (43:12), and He said: وَعَلَيْها وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ — and you are carried on them and on ships. (40:80).

Some have interpreted the "ship" mentioned in the previous verse as the Ark of Noah (peace be upon him), and what is in this verse as the ships and boats made after it; this is a poor interpretation. Similar to it is the interpretation of what is in this verse as camels specifically.

Perhaps what is in this verse can be interpreted as airplanes and airships made in these times; generalization is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ نَشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْقَذُونَ — And if We wish, We drown them: then they have no one to help them, nor are they rescued, (36:43). The helper (الصريخ) is the one who answers the cry and aids the one seeking help. Rescuing (الإنقاذ) is saving from drowning.

The verse is connected to His previous saying: أَنَّا حَمَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ — that We carried their progeny in the laden ship — meaning: the matter is subject to Our will; if We wish, We drown them, and no helper shall aid them, nor shall any rescuer save them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَمَتاعاً إِلى حِينٍ — except as a mercy from Us and an enjoyment for a while. (36:44). This is an elliptical exception (استثناء مفرغ), and the estimation is: they are not saved by any cause or any matter except due to a mercy from Us that reaches them, and for enjoyment until the time of the appointed term We have ordained for them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ — And when they are told, ‘Beware of what is before you and what is behind you, so that you may receive [His] mercy...’ (36:45). Since He mentioned the signs pointing to Lordship, He censured them for not observing its right, not turning toward it, and not acting upon its implications. So when it is said to them: "These clear signs speak that your Lord is Allah, so beware of disobeying Him in your present state and the sins you have previously committed," or "the punishment for polytheism and sins with which you are afflicted and what you have left behind you," or "beware of what is before you of polytheism and sins in the life of this world and what is behind you of punishment in the Hereafter," they turned away from it and did not respond to it, as is their habit with all the signs they are reminded of.

From here, it becomes clear, firstly: that what is intended by "what is before them and what is behind them" is the polytheism and sins they are afflicted with in their present state and what they were afflicted with before; or the punishment they have deserved thereby—and the outcome is the same; or polytheism and sins in this world and punishment in the Hereafter, which is the most plausible of views.

Secondly: that the omission of the apodosis (جواب) of idha (when) is to indicate that their state has reached such a level of audacity against Allah and belittling of the Truth that it is impossible to mention how they would answer the caller to Truth when he calls them to godwariness (التقوى); thus it must be left out of sorrow and not mentioned. This has been indicated by His saying: وَما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ — Not a sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord but that they are disregardful of it. (36:46).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ — Not a sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord but that they are disregardful of it. (36:46). What is intended by the "coming" of the signs is their reaching them through observation or through recitation and mention. Also, it is more general than being a sign in the horizons or within the souls, or being a miraculous sign like the Qur'an; they are disregardful of all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ — And when they are told, ‘Spend out of what Allah has provided you,’ (36:47) until the end of the verse. His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ dealt with their response when called to the worship of Allah, which is one of the two pillars of the true religion. This verse deals with their response when called to compassion for the creation of Allah, which is the other pillar. It is known that their response is rejection rather than acceptance.

Thus, His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ includes a call to them to spend on the poor and the destitute from their wealth. In expressing wealth as "what Allah has provided you," there is an intimation that the true owner of it is Allah, who provided it to them and gave them authority over it. He is the one who created the poor and the destitute and established their need for the surplus provisions these people possess, which they do not lack. So let them spend on them and act with excellence and beauty, for Allah loves excellence (الإحسان) and beautiful action.

His saying: قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنُطْعِمُ مَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ أَطْعَمَهُ — the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Shall we feed him whom Allah would have fed, had He wished? (36:47) is their response to the call to spend. The speaker was explicitly mentioned as "those who disbelieved" (الذين كفروا)—whereas the context would normally require a pronoun—to indicate that their disbelief in the Truth and their turning away from it by following desires is what prompted them to offer such an excuse, based on turning away from what the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — calls for of compassion for Allah's creation and rectifying what is corrupted in society. Likewise, the explicit mention in His saying "to those who believed" (للذين آمنوا) is to indicate that the ones who said "Spend out of what Allah has provided you" were the believers.

In their saying: أَنُطْعِمُ مَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ أَطْعَمَهُ — Shall we feed him whom Allah would have fed, had He wished? — there is an intimation that the believers only said to them: "Spend out of what Allah has provided you" on the grounds that it is something Allah wishes and desires as a religious ruling. So they rejected it by arguing that Allah's will does not fail to achieve its object; thus, if He wished to feed them, He would have fed them—that is, He would have increased their sustenance and made them wealthy.

This is a fallacy (مغالطة) on their part, in which they confused the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية)—which is based on trial, examination, and guiding servants to what contains the benefit of their state in their world and their Hereafter, and which may fail to achieve its object through disobedience—with the creative will (الإرادة التكوينية), which never fails to achieve its object. It is well known that Allah's wish and will concerning feeding the poor and spending on them is of the legislative wish, not the creative one. Thus, its failure to be realized in the case of the poor only points to the disobedience of those who disbelieved and their rebellion against what they were commanded, not to the lack of the will's attachment to it or the falsehood of the one claiming it.

This is a fallacy upon which they built most of the pagan traditions they fabricated. Allah, the Glorified, has related this from them in His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him.’ (16:35); and His saying: سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْءٍ — The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partners [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’ (6:148); and His saying: وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ — They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ (43:20).

His saying: إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — You are only in manifest error. (36:47) is part of the speech of those who disbelieved, addressing the believers; meaning: "You are in manifest error in your claim that Allah commanded us to spend and wished that from us."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', it is narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin, Abu Ja'far al-Baqir, and Ja'far al-Sadiq (peace be upon them): لا مستقر لها (having no resting place for it) with the ra in the accusative case.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abu Dharr, who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها (And the sun runs on to its resting place). He said: 'Its resting place is beneath the Throne.'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated from Abu Dharr from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, in both brief and lengthy versions. In some of them, it is mentioned that after sunset, it ascends heaven by heaven until it reaches beneath the Throne, where it prostrates and seeks permission to rise, remaining so until it is clothed in light and permitted to rise.

If the narration is authentic, it must be interpreted figuratively.

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain to Sallam ibn al-Mustanir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created the sun before the moon, and He created light before darkness."

In al-Majma', al-Ayyashi narrated in his Tafsir with a chain to al-Ash'ath ibn Hatim, who said: "I was in Khurasan when al-Rida (peace be upon him), al-Fadl ibn Sahl, and al-Ma'mun gathered in the pavilion at Marw. The table was set, and al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'A man from the Children of Israel asked me in Medina, saying: Was the day created before or the night? What do you have to say?' He said: 'They discussed the matter, but they had nothing to say regarding it.'"

"Then al-Fadl said to al-Rida: 'Inform us of it, may Allah rectify you.' He said: 'Yes. From the Qur'an or from calculation?' Al-Fadl said to him: 'From the perspective of calculation.' He said: 'You know, O Fadl, that the ascendant of the world is Cancer, and the planets were in their positions of exaltation: Saturn in Libra, Jupiter in Cancer, Mars in Capricorn, the Sun in Aries, Venus in Pisces, Mercury in Virgo, and the Moon in Taurus. Thus, the sun would be in the tenth house, the midheaven; so the day is before the night. And from the Qur'an is His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ (nor may the night outrun the day), meaning the night has been preceded by the day.'"

I say: Al-Alusi quoted this hadith in Ruh al-Ma'ani, then said: "In the inference from the verse, there is an obvious discussion. As for calculation, it has a basis in general, and the astronomers held that the beginning of the cycle is the meridian circle, which accords with what was mentioned. What is most likely is that the report is not authentic from its origin, for al-Rida is too exalted to infer from the verse what you have heard of his claim." End quote.

The matter became confused for him in grasping the reality of the meaning of night and day.

To clarify: Night and day are opposites in the sense of privation and possession (العدم والملكة), like blindness and sight. Just as blindness is not the absolute absence of sight—such that a wall, for instance, would be blind because of the absence of sight in it—but rather it is the absence of sight in that which is naturally disposed to possess sight, like a human being; likewise, night is not the absolute absence of light. Rather, it is the time of the absence of illumination of a region of the earth by the light of the sun. It is well known that the realization of privation (عدم الملكة) depends on the prior realization of the corresponding possession (الملكة) so that it may be defined in relation to it. If there were no sight, blindness would not be realized; and if there were no day, night would not be realized.

Thus, absolute night, in the sense by which it is night, is preceded in existence by day. His saying: وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ (nor may the night outrun the day), although it looks at the assumed order between the days and nights—that there is a day, then a night, then a day, then a night—and that none of these nights precedes the day adjacent to it;

Nevertheless, He, the Exalted, took in His saying وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ absolute night and negated its precedence over absolute day. He did not say that one of the nights occurring in this order does not precede the day occurring in the order before it.

Thus, the judgment in the verse is based on what the nature of night and day requires according to the opposition that Allah has placed between them. From this, the judgment of the preservation of the order in the alternation of night and day has been derived, for every night is the absence of the day that it follows, so it does not precede it. To this, he (peace be upon him) points after mentioning the verse by his saying: "meaning the night has been preceded by the day," meaning that the day's preceding the night is its being created before it, and it is not as imagined that there are days and nights already existing and then a place is assigned for each of them.

As for the objector's saying: "As for calculation, it has a basis in general," it is not known why he said "in general," for it is a complete basis built upon the acceptance of the principles of astronomy, correct "in total" (بالجملة) on that assumption, not "in general" (في الجملة).

Likewise, his saying: "and the astronomers held that the beginning of the cycle is the meridian circle, which accords with what was mentioned," is of no avail. This is because the meridian circle—which is the circle passing through the two poles and a third point between them—is infinite in number and no specific point in the sky is assigned to it over another point, such that the sun being in one of them would be day for the earth and not the other.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ: Al-Halabi narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Its meaning is: Beware of what is before you of sins, and what is behind you of punishment."

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٨)
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, should you be truthful?’ (48)
مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ (٤٩)
They do not await but a single Cry that will seize them as they wrangle. (49)
فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٥٠)
Then they will not be able to make any will, nor will they return to their folks. (50)
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنسِلُونَ (٥١)
And when the Trumpet is blown, behold, there they will be, scrambling towards their Lord from their graves! (51)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا مَنۢ بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا ۜ ۗ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَصَدَقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٥٢)
They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep?’ ‘This is what the All-beneficent had promised, and the apostles had spoken the truth!’ (52)
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (٥٣)
It will be but a single Cry, and, behold, they will all be presented before Us! (53)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٤)
‘Today no soul will be wronged in the least, nor will you be requited except for what you used to do.’ (54)
إِنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى شُغُلٍ فَـٰكِهُونَ (٥٥)
Indeed today the inhabitants of paradise rejoice in their engagements (55)
هُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُمْ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ مُتَّكِـُٔونَ (٥٦)
—they and their mates, reclining on couches in the shades. (56)
لَهُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَلَهُم مَّا يَدَّعُونَ (٥٧)
There they have fruits, and they have whatever they want. (57)
سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْلًا مِّن رَّبٍّ رَّحِيمٍ (٥٨)
‘Peace!’—a watchword from the all-merciful Lord. (58)
وَٱمْتَـٰزُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٥٩)
And ‘Get apart today, you guilty ones!’ (59)
۞ أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٦٠)
‘Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. (60)
وَأَنِ ٱعْبُدُونِى ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦١)
Worship Me. That is a straight path”? (61)
وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا ۖ أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٢)
Certainly, he has led astray many of your generations. Have you not exercised your reason? (62)
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (٦٣)
This is the hell you had been promised! (63)
ٱصْلَوْهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٦٤)
Enter it today, because of what you used to deny. (64)
ٱلْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلَىٰٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنَآ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٦٥)
Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness concerning what they used to earn.’ (65)

(Commentary)

Having concluded the detailed exposition of the verses of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — which were referred to summarily at the beginning of the discourse, He begins the detailed account of the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of creation to Allah for judgment. He mentions the manner in which the Hour will commence, the gathering of people for reckoning (الحساب) and recompense (الجزاء), and what the inhabitants of Paradise and the criminals will be rewarded with. All of this serves as an elucidation of the previously summarized report concerning the Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ "They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’" (36:48). This is a statement from them issued by way of mockery and based on denial. Perhaps for this reason, the demonstrative pronoun for the near (هذا) was used, and because the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers frequently spoke to them about the Day of Resurrection and warned them of it. The term promise (الوعد) is used for both good and evil when mentioned alone; however, when contrasted with threat (الوعيد), "promise" (الوعد) is specified for good and "threat" (الوعيد) for evil.

His saying, the Exalted: ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ "They do not await but a single shout that will seize them while they are [still] disputing." (36:49). Awaiting (النظر) here carries the meaning of waiting (الانتظار). What is meant by the "shout" (الصيحة) is the first blast of the Trumpet, as aided by the context. Describing the shout as "single" (واحدة) points to the insignificance of their affair to Allah—exalted is His greatness—as it requires no additional effort. The word يَخِصِّمُونَ is originally yakhtasimun (يختصمون), derived from disputation (الاختصام), meaning arguing and quarreling.

The verse is a response to their saying: "When will this promise be fulfilled?" It is phrased in a manner that mocks them and belittles their situation, just as their own statement was. The meaning is: these speakers who ask "When will this promise be fulfilled?"—a question that implies they are waiting—await nothing but a single shout, which is easy for Us without effort or burden. It will seize them such that they will have no capacity to flee or escape from it, while they are heedless of it, disputing among themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلا إِلى أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "Then they will not be able to make any bequest, nor will they return to their families." (36:50). That is, it follows from this shout—inasmuch as it surprises them and grants them no respite—that they die instantly. Thus, they cannot make a bequest (توصية)—especially since death encompasses them all at once, leaving no one to whom a bequest could be made—nor can they return to their families if they were, for example, outside their homes.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ "And the Trumpet will be blown, and behold, they will be rushing from the graves toward their Lord." (36:51). This is the second blast of the Trumpet, by which life and resurrection (البعث) occur. Graves (الأجداث) is the plural of jadath (جدث), meaning the tomb. Rushing (النسل) means walking quickly. Expressing it as "toward their Lord" (إِلى رَبِّهِمْ) is a rebuke to them because they used to deny His Lordship (الربوبية). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا هذا ما وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ "They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep? This is what the All-beneficent promised, and the apostles spoke the truth!’" (36:52). Raising (البعث) means making one stand up. Place of sleep (المرقد) refers to the place of slumber, meaning the grave. Their referring to Him, the Exalted, as "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is a form of seeking mercy, whereas in the world they used to say: "And what is the All-Beneficent?" (25:60). His saying: "and the apostles spoke the truth" is conjoined to His saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised"; a verbal sentence may be conjoined to a nominal one.

Their saying: "Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep?" is based on their denial of the Resurrection while they were in the world, and the deep-rooted effect of that denial and heedlessness regarding the Day of Recompense in their souls. They remain immersed in their desires; thus, when they rise from their graves rushing toward the Gathering (المحشر), they are suddenly confronted with entry into a realm where nothing awaits them but the expectation of evil. They are seized by the Great Terror (الفزع الأكبر) and a bewilderment that even mountains could not withstand. Therefore, they first hasten to cry out for woe and destruction, as was their habit in the world when falling into perils. Then they ask who raised them from their place of sleep because the bewilderment surrounding them made them oblivious to everything.

Then they recall what the Messengers (عليه‌السلام) used to remind them of regarding the true promise of Resurrection and Recompense. They testify to the truth of the promise and seek refuge in mercy, saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised," following their habit in the world where they would flatter their enemy if he overcame them, showing humiliation and admitting to wrongdoing and negligence. Then they confirm the Messengers by saying: "and the apostles spoke the truth."

From what has preceded, the reason for their cry of woe upon being raised becomes clear. Secondly, the reason for their question about who raised them from their place of sleep—which suggests they were initially ignorant of it—becomes clear, followed by their admission that it is what the All-Beneficent promised and their confirmation of the Messengers in what they conveyed from Him, the Exalted. It also becomes clear that the saying: "Who raised us from our place of sleep..." etc., and the saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." etc., are both part of their speech.

It has been said that the phrase "and the apostles spoke the truth" is conjoined to the object of "what" (ما), where "what" is either a relative pronoun or an infinitive-forming particle, and that "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." etc., is a response from Allah, the angels, or the believers to their question: "Who raised us from our place of sleep?" It is not hidden that this is contrary to the apparent meaning, especially if "what" (ما) is taken as an infinitive-forming particle. Furthermore, if the phrase "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." were a response from Allah or the angels to their question "Who raised us from our place of sleep?", the response would have identified the agent (the doer) rather than the act, because they asked about the agent of the raising! The claim that the shift was intended to remind them of their disbelief and rebuke them for it, while implicitly pointing to the agent, is of no significant value.

It also becomes clear that "This is what the All-Beneficent promised" consists of a subject and a predicate. It has been said that "this" (هذا) is an adjective for "our place of sleep" (مرقدنا) by interpreting the demonstrative pronoun as a derivative, and that "what" (ما) is a subject whose predicate is omitted, estimated as "what the All-Beneficent promised is true." This is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ كانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً فَإِذا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنا مُحْضَرُونَ "It will be but a single shout, and behold, they will all be brought before Us." (36:53). The subject of kānat (كانت) is omitted, and the estimation is: "The event or the blast was nothing but a single blast," which surprises them such that they are gathered and brought before Us without delay or respite. The expression "before Us" (لَدَيْنا) is used because the day is the Day of Presence for the final judgment before Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْيَوْمَ لا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً وَلا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ "Today no soul shall be wronged in the least, nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do." (36:54). That is, on this day, He judges between them with a just judgment and decrees with a truthful decree; thus, no soul shall be wronged in the least.

His saying: "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" is an explanatory conjunction to His saying: "Today no soul shall be wronged in the least." It is, in reality, a demonstrative proof for the negation of injustice on that day, as it indicates that the recompense for the deeds of the doers on that day is the very deeds themselves. Injustice (الظلم) — the act of placing something in other than its proper place — is inconceivable in such a case, because burdening the doer with his own deed is necessarily placing a thing in its proper place.

The address in the verse is by way of representing the Day of Resurrection and making it and those within it present through linguistic attention. It is not—as some have imagined—a narration of what will be said to them or how they will be addressed by Allah, the Glorified, the angels, or the believers on the Day of Resurrection; there is no requirement for this from the context. The addressees of His saying: "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" are both the felicitous and the wretched.

As for the objection that the restriction (الحصر) precludes generalization—since He, the Exalted, pays the believers their rewards in full and increases them manifold from His grace—it is refuted by the fact that the restriction in the verse pertains to the recompense and reward for the deed itself. Verses indicating an "increase," such as His saying: "They will have whatever they wish therein, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35), refer to a matter beyond recompense and reward, outside the scope of the deed.

It has also been answered that the meaning of the verse is that the righteous person's reward will not be diminished, and the wicked person's punishment will not be increased, as wisdom would contradict that. However, increasing the reward or decreasing the punishment is not prohibited. Alternatively, the meaning of "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" is that you will only be requited with the kind of your deed: if good, then good; if evil, then evil. However, if the meaning of the verse were as mentioned, the problem would be resolved, but the issue lies in whether the verse actually indicates that.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ الْيَوْمَ فِي شُغُلٍ فاكِهُونَ "Indeed today the inhabitants of paradise are happily occupied." (36:55). Occupation (الشغل) is an affair that occupies a person and diverts them from everything else. Happy (الفاكه) is derived from fakāhah (الفكاهة), which is talking about what pleases or enjoying and taking delight; it has no triliteral root verb, according to what has been said.

It has been said that فاكِهُونَ means "possessors of fruit" (ذوو فاكهة), similar to lābin (possessor of milk) and tāmir (possessor of dates). This is unlikely because fruit (الفاكهة) is mentioned in the context, and there is no reason for its repetition. The meaning is that the inhabitants of Paradise on this day are in an affair that occupies them from everything else, which is the enjoyment of Paradise, taking delight therein.

His saying, the Exalted: هُمْ وَأَزْواجُهُمْ فِي ظِلالٍ عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ مُتَّكِؤُنَ "They and their spouses will be in shades, reclining on couches." (36:56). Shades (الظلال) is the plural of zill (ظل), and it is said to be the plural of zullah (ظلة), which is a covering from the sun, whether a roof, trees, or otherwise. Couch (الأريكة) is anything one reclines upon, such as a cushion or similar. The meaning is: they—the inhabitants of Paradise—and their spouses from their believing wives in the world or from the Hūr al-ʿĪn, are in shades or coverings from the sun and other things, reclining on couches as the reclining of the honored.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ فِيها فاكِهَةٌ وَلَهُمْ ما يَدَّعُونَ "There they will have fruits and they will have whatever they call for." (36:57). Fruit (الفاكهة) refers to the fruits one enjoys, such as apples, citrons, and the like. His saying: يَدَّعُونَ is from iddiʿā’ (الادعاء) in the sense of wishing; meaning, they have in Paradise fruit and they have therein whatever they wish for and request.

His saying, the Exalted: سَلامٌ قَوْلاً مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ "‘Peace!’—a watchword from a merciful Lord." (36:58). "Peace" (سلام) is a subject whose predicate is omitted, and the indefiniteness is for magnification (التفخيم); the estimation is "Peace be upon them" or "For them is peace." "A watchword" (قَوْلاً) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, the estimation being: "I say it as a word from a Merciful Lord."

It is apparent that the "Peace" is from Him, the Exalted, and it is other than the peace of the angels mentioned in His saying: "and the angels will enter upon them from every gate: ‘Peace be to you, for your patience.’ How excellent is the ultimate abode!" (13:24).

His saying, the Exalted: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ "‘Get apart today, O you guilty ones!’" (36:59). That is, We say today to the criminals: "Separate yourselves from the inhabitants of Paradise." This is their segregation from them on the Day of Resurrection and the fulfillment of what He said in another place: "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:28), and His saying: "Do those who commit vices suppose that We shall make them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal?" (45:21).

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy." (36:60). Exhortation (العهد) means a command or testament. What is meant by the "worship of Satan" is obeying him in what he whispers and commands, for there is no obedience except to Allah or to one whose obedience He has commanded. The prohibition of obeying him is justified by his being a manifest enemy, for an enemy does not desire good for his foe.

It has been said that what is meant by his worship is the worship of idols besides Allah, and it is attributed to Satan because it occurs through his enticement and beautification; however, this is an unnecessary affectation. The address is directed to the criminals with the title "Children of Adam" because the enmity of Satan first took root with Adam when he was commanded to prostrate to him but refused and was arrogant, thus being stoned. He then returned to his progeny with enmity and threatened them, as Allah, the Exalted, narrated when he said: "He said, ‘Do You see this one whom You have honored above me? If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, [all] but a few.’" (17:62).

As for His covenant (العهد) and testament to the Children of Adam not to obey him, it is what He exhorted them with through the tongues of His Messengers and Prophets, warning them against following him, such as His saying: "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you as he expelled your parents from paradise" (7:27), and His saying: "And do not let Satan bar you [from the right path]. Indeed he is your manifest enemy." (43:62).

It has been said that what is meant by the covenant is His covenant with them in the Realm of Particles (عالم الذر) when He said: "‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes.’" (7:172). You have already learned from what we presented in the commentary on the verse of the Particles that the covenant there is, in one respect, the very same covenant directed to them in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ "‘And worship Me. This is a straight path’?" (36:61). This is an explanatory conjunction to what preceded it. Discussion regarding the meaning of the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — has already passed in the commentary on His saying: "Guide us on the straight path" in Surah al-Fatihah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنْكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيراً أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْقِلُونَ "He has certainly led astray a great multitude of you. Did you not use to apply reason?" (36:62). Multitude (الجبل) means a group, and it is said to mean a large group. The speech is based on rebuke and censure.

His saying, the Exalted: هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ "This is the hell you were promised." (36:63). That is, the threat of it continued to be issued to you time after time through the tongues of the Prophets and Messengers (عليه‌السلام). The first time Allah, the Glorified, threatened with it was when He said to Iblis: "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all." (15:42-43). The wording of the verse points to the bringing forth of Hell on that day.

His saying, the Exalted: اصْلَوْهَا الْيَوْمَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ "Enter it today because of your disbelief." (36:64). Entering (الصلا) means remaining and following; it is also said to mean enduring heat. It appears from His saying: "because of your disbelief" that the address is to the disbelievers, and they are the ones meant by "the guilty ones" (المجرمين).

His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ "Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness to what they used to earn." (36:65). That is, each of them will bear witness to what they used to earn through its medium: the hands regarding the sins they committed with them, and the feet regarding the sins specific to them, as the context suggests.

From here it becomes clear that every limb will speak regarding the deed specific to it, and that the mention of hands and feet is by way of example. For this reason, in another place, hearing, sight, and the heart are mentioned, as in Surah al-Isra, verse 36. In another place, the skins are mentioned, as in Surah Fussilat, verse 20. Some related discussion will follow in the commentary on Surah Fussilat, if Allah wills.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً — the verse — he said: "That is at the end of time; a shout will be cried out among them—while they are in their markets disputing—and they will all die in their places. Not one of them will return to his home, nor will he make a bequest. That is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلا إِلى أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ."

In al-Majmaʿ, in a hadith: تقوم الساعة والرجلان قد نشرا ثوبهما يتبايعان ـ فما يطويانه حتى تقوم الساعة ، والرجل يرفع أكلته إلى فيه حتى تقوم الساعة ، والرجل يليط حوضه ليسقي ماشيته ـ فما يسقيها حتى تقوم — "The Hour will commence while two men have spread out their garment to trade—they will not fold it before the Hour commences. A man will raise his morsel to his mouth, and the Hour will commence. A man will be plastering (يليط) his trough to water his livestock—he will not water them before it commences."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and likewise from Qatadah from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a disconnected (مرسلا) report.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ — he said: "From the graves." In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا — "The people were in the graves; when they rose, they thought they had been asleep and said: يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا. The angels said: هذا ما وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Abu Dharr (may Allah have mercy on him) used to say in his sermon: 'Between death and resurrection is but like a sleep you slept and then woke up from.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ الْيَوْمَ فِي شُغُلٍ فاكِهُونَ — he said: "They joke with women and play with them."

In the same, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: فِي ظِلالٍ عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ مُتَّكِؤُنَ — "The couches (الأرائك) are beds with canopies over them."

In the same, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: سَلامٌ قَوْلاً مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ — he said: "The Peace from Him is security (الأمان)." Regarding His saying: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — he said: "When Allah gathers the creation on the Day of Resurrection, they will remain standing on their feet until sweat bridles them. They will cry out: 'O Lord, settle our account, even if it be to the Fire!' He said: 'Then Allah will send winds that will strike between them, and a caller will cry out: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ. So He will separate them; the criminals will go to the Fire, and whoever had faith in his heart will go to Paradise.'"

I say: It has been mentioned in some narrations that Allah, the Glorified, manifests Himself to them, and they become occupied with Him to the exclusion of all else as long as the manifestation (التجلي) lasts. What is meant by it is the lifting of every veil between them and their Lord, not visual seeing (الرؤية البصرية), which cannot occur except with directions and dimensions, for these are impossible in His regard, the Exalted.

In the Iʿtiqadat of al-Saduq, he (peace be upon him) said: من أصغى إلى ناطق فقد عبده ـ فإن كان الناطق عن الله فقد عبد الله ، وإن كان الناطق عن إبليس فقد عبد إبليس — "Whoever listens to a speaker has worshipped him; if the speaker speaks from Allah, he has worshipped Allah, and if the speaker speaks from Iblis, he has worshipped Iblis."

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Salim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "The limbs do not bear witness against a believer; they only bear witness against one for whom the word of punishment has become due. As for the believer, he is given his book in his right hand. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَأُولئِكَ يَقْرَؤُنَ كِتابَهُمْ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً — 'As for those who are given their record in their right hand—they will read their record, and they will not be wronged so much as a peach-pit fiber.'" (17:71).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his grandfather, he said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said in a sermon describing the horror of the Day of Resurrection: 'Allah will seal the mouths so they do not speak, and the hands will speak, and the feet will bear witness, and the skins will speak of what they did; thus they will not hide any discourse from Allah.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, some of which will come under the commentary on His saying: "their hearing, their sight, and their skins will bear witness against them" (41:20), and some have already passed in the discussion on His saying: "Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable." (17:36).

Ayahs

وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَطَمَسْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَٱسْتَبَقُوا۟ ٱلصِّرَٰطَ فَأَنَّىٰ يُبْصِرُونَ (٦٦)
Had We wished We would have blotted out their eyes: then, were they to advance towards the path, how would have they seen? (66)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَمَسَخْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِهِمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مُضِيًّا وَلَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٦٧)
And had We wished We would have deformed them in their place; then they would neither have been able to move ahead nor to return. (67)
وَمَن نُّعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ ۖ أَفَلَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٨)
And whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation. Then, will they not exercise their reason? (68)
وَمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ ٱلشِّعْرَ وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْءَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (٦٩)
We did not teach him poetry, nor does it behoove him. This is just a reminder and a manifest Quran, (69)
لِّيُنذِرَ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٠)
so that anyone who is alive may be warned, and that the word may come due against the faithless. (70)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا فَهُمْ لَهَا مَـٰلِكُونَ (٧١)
Have they not seen that We have created for them—of what Our hands have worked—cattle, so they have become their masters? (71)
وَذَلَّلْنَـٰهَا لَهُمْ فَمِنْهَا رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْهَا يَأْكُلُونَ (٧٢)
And We made them tractable for them; so some of them make their mounts and some of them they eat. (72)
وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَمَشَارِبُ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
There are other benefits for them therein, and drinks. Will they not then give thanks? (73)
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٧٤)
They have taken gods besides Allah, [hoping] that they might be helped [by the fake deities]. (74)
لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُندٌ مُّحْضَرُونَ (٧٥)
[But] they cannot help them, while they [themselves] are an army mobilized for their defence. (75)
فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٦)
So do not let their remarks grieve you. We indeed know whatever they hide and whatever they disclose. (76)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٧)
Does not man see that We created him from a drop of [seminal] fluid, and, behold, he is an open contender!? (77)
وَضَرَبَ لَنَا مَثَلًا وَنَسِىَ خَلْقَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ مَن يُحْىِ ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ وَهِىَ رَمِيمٌ (٧٨)
He draws comparisons for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’ (78)
قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَهَآ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ (٧٩)
Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time, and He has knowledge of all creation. (79)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ ٱلْأَخْضَرِ نَارًا فَإِذَآ أَنتُم مِّنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ (٨٠)
He, who made for you fire out of the green tree, and, behold, you light fire from it! (80)
أَوَلَيْسَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَهُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨١)
Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the like of them? Yes indeed! He is the All-creator, the All-knowing. (81)
إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُۥٓ إِذَآ أَرَادَ شَيْـًٔا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٨٢)
All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. (82)
فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٣)
So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things, and to whom you shall be brought back. (83)

(Commentary)

This is a summary exposition of the preceding meanings in a different context. It contains a threat of punishment for them, an indication that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a Messenger and that his Book is a Reminder (الذكر) and a Qur’an—not the work of a poet nor is his Book poetry—an allusion to the creation of cattle as a sign (آية) of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and an argumentation for the Resurrection (الميعاد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَطَمَسْنا عَلى أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ فَأَنَّى يُبْصِرُونَ (Had We wished, We would have surely effaced their eyes, and they would have raced for the path. Then how could they have seen? 36:66). It is stated in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan: "Effacement (الطمس) is the erasing of a thing until its trace disappears. Thus, effacing the eye is like effacing a book, and similarly, the effacing of wealth is its removal such that it can no longer be perceived. A blind person is called effaced (مطموس) or obliterated (طميس) when the slit between the eyelids disappears." End quote.

Thus, His saying: وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَطَمَسْنا عَلى أَعْيُنِهِمْ means: Had We willed, We would have removed their eyes so they would become smooth with no trace left, thereby taking away their sight and nullifying their vision.

His saying: فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ means: They would have intended to race toward the clear path (الصراط) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — which one who seeks it does not miss and one who travels it does not stray from, yet they would not have seen it and would never see it. The sense of impossibility understood from His saying: فَأَنَّى يُبْصِرُونَ (Then how could they have seen?) is a metaphor (كناية) for total prevention.

The view of some that "racing for the path" refers to their rushing to follow the path of truth and their failure to be guided to it is not without some far-fetchedness (بعد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَمَسَخْناهُمْ عَلى مَكانَتِهِمْ فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مُضِيًّا وَلا يَرْجِعُونَ (And had We wished, We would have surely transformed them in their places; then they would be able neither to move ahead nor to return. 36:67). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "Metamorphosis (المسخ) is the turning of a form into a disfigured creation, just as a people were transformed into apes and swine." He also said: "Place (المكانة) and location (المكان) are the same." End quote. The meaning of transforming them "in their places" is the disfigurement of their creation while they are sitting in the very spot they occupy, without changing them from their state through any treatment or exertion, but by mere Will (المشية). This is a metaphor for it being effortless and easy for Him, the Exalted, without any difficulty.

His saying: فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مُضِيًّا وَلا يَرْجِعُونَ means: They could not move forward (مضياً) into the punishment, nor could they return to their state prior to the punishment and metamorphosis. Thus, "moving forward" and "returning" are metaphors for returning to a state of well-being or remaining in a state of punishment and metamorphosis.

It has been said that it refers to their moving toward their objectives and returning to their homes and families, but this is not without some far-fetchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ نُعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِي الْخَلْقِ أَفَلا يَعْقِلُونَ (Whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation. Do they not exercise their reason? 36:68). Granting long life (التعمير) is the lengthening of one's lifespan, and regression (التنكيس) is the overturning of a thing such that its top becomes its bottom, and its strength turns into weakness and its increase into decrease. A human being in the stage of senility is regressed in creation; his strength turns to weakness, his knowledge to ignorance, and his memory to forgetfulness.

The verse is in the position of citing the regression of creation as evidence for the possibility of the content of the two preceding verses. The meaning is that He who regresses the creation of a human being when He grants him long life is capable of effacing their eyes and transforming them in their places.

In His saying: أَفَلا يَعْقِلُونَ (Do they not exercise their reason?), there is a rebuke for their lack of intellection and an urging for them to contemplate these matters and take heed from them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ (We did not teach him poetry, nor does it behoove him. This is just a reminder and a manifest Qur’an, 36:69). This is a conjunction and a return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah regarding the confirmation of the Messenger's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) message and that his Book is a revelation (تنزيل) from Him, the Exalted.

Thus, His saying: وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ (We did not teach him poetry) negates that He taught him poetry. The necessary implication is that he is such that he does not compose poetry well—not that he is capable of it but refrains from it due to a prohibition from Allah directed at him, nor that the revealed Qur’an is not poetry even if he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were able to compose it.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ (nor does it behoove him) is in the position of bestowing a favor upon him by purifying him from composing poetry. The sentence serves to repel a potential objection. The gist is that Our not teaching him poetry does not entail a deficiency in him, nor is it an incapacitation; rather, it is to elevate his rank and to sanctify his presence from that which besets one who is skilled in the craft of poetry—falling into the trap of decorating meanings with false poetic imaginings (التخيلات الشعرية الكاذبة) which, the more they are delved into, the more the speech affects the soul, and organizing speech with musical meters to make it more pleasing to the ear. Thus, it does not behoove him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to compose poetry while he is a Messenger from Allah, and the sign of his message and the core of his call is the Qur’an, which is miraculous in its eloquence—a Reminder (الذكر) and a manifest Qur’an.

His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ (This is just a reminder and a manifest Qur’an) is an explanation and clarification of His saying: وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ, since its necessary meaning is that the Qur’an is not poetry. The restriction (الحصر) understood from His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ etc., is a "restriction of reversal" (قصر القلب); the meaning is: It is not poetry; it is nothing but a Reminder and a manifest Qur’an.

The meaning of it being a "Reminder" and a "Qur’an" is that it is a Reminder recited from Allah; this is its apparent sense.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُنْذِرَ مَنْ كانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ (so that he may warn him who is alive, and that the word may come true against the faithless. 36:70). This is a causal clause (تعليل) connected to His saying: وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ. The meaning is: We did not teach him poetry so that he may warn, by means of the Qur’an—which is purified from being poetry—him who is alive, etc. Or it is connected to His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ etc., meaning: That which he recites to the people is nothing but a Reminder and a manifest Qur’an which We sent down to him so that he may warn him who is alive, etc. The outcome of both views is the same.

The verse—as you see—considers the ultimate purpose of sending the Messenger and revealing the Qur’an to be the warning of him who is alive—which is a metaphor for one who intellectually perceives the truth and hears it—and the realization and necessity of the Word against the disbelievers (الكافرين) — those who reject the truth after it has become manifest. The correspondence of this verse to the verses at the beginning of the Surah regarding this meaning is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا أَنْعاماً فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ (Have they not seen that We have created for them cattle from what Our hands have wrought, and they are their masters? 36:71). This mentions one of the signs of monotheism (التوحيد) that points to His Lordship (الربوبية) and His governance (التدبير) of the human world. It is similar to what preceded among the earlier signs of monotheism, such as the reviving of the dead earth by bringing forth grain and fruits and the gushing of springs.

The meaning of the cattle being "from what Our hands have wrought" is the absence of any partner in their creation and its exclusivity to Him, the Exalted. Thus, the "work of the hands" is a metaphor for exclusivity (الاختصاص).

His saying: فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ (and they are their masters) is a derivation from His saying: خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ (We have created for them), for the meaning is: We created them for their sake. Thus, they are created for the sake of the human being, and the necessary consequence is their exclusivity to him. This exclusivity culminates in ownership (الملك), for the conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) found in society is a branch of exclusivity.

By this, the weakness in the statement of some becomes clear, who said that the derivation of فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ from خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ is obscure, and that it is apparently derived from an omitted phrase, the estimation being: "We created them for them, so they are their owners." You are well aware that its derivation from خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ is not obscure and there is no need for such an estimation.

It has also been said that "ownership" (الملك) here means power and subjugation, but this is refuted by the fact that this is understood from the subsequent verse: وَذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ (And We have subdued them for them), and establishing a new meaning (التأسيس) is better than mere emphasis (التأكيد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ فَمِنْها رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ (And We have subdued them for them, so some of them they ride and some of them they eat. 36:72). Subduing the cattle means making them compliant and non-rebellious, which is their subjugation (تسخير) for them. The mount (الركوب)—with a fatha on the ra—refers to beasts of burden like camels and oxen. His saying: وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ means they eat from their meat.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ وَمَشارِبُ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ (And in them they have [other] benefits and drinks. Will they not then give thanks? 36:73). The "benefits" (المنافع) refer to what they utilize of their hair, wool, skins, and so on. Drinks (المشارب) is the plural of mashrab—a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) used in the sense of the object—referring to milk. The discussion regarding the meaning of gratitude (الشكر) is the same as what preceded in His saying: وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ.

The meaning of these three verses is: Have they not known that We created for their sake and for the governance of their worldly life cattle—camels, oxen, and sheep—and it followed from that that they are their owners with an ownership that validates all types of their disposal over them without any opponent? And We subdued them for them by making them subjugated and compliant, not rebellious; so from them is their mount which they ride, and from them—meaning from their meat—they eat. And they have in them benefits, utilizing their hair, wool, and skins, and drinks from their milk which they drink. Will they not then thank Allah for this perfect governance which reveals His Lordship over them? Or will they not worship Him in gratitude for His favors?

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ آلِهَةً لَعَلَّهُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ (They have taken gods besides Allah, [hoping] that they might be helped. 36:74). The plural pronouns refer to the polytheists. The "gods" (آلهة) refer to idols, or devils, or human tyrants (فراعنة البشر), excluding the near-stationed angels and the saintly humans, because the end of the passage—وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ—does not suit them.

They only took them as gods in the hope that they might be helped by them, because the common folk among them take a god under the assumption that the management of their affairs—whether good or evil—is delegated to the one they have taken as a god. Thus, the worshiper among them worships it to please it with his worship, so that it does not become angry and cut off the blessing or send down a calamity.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ (They cannot help them, yet they are an army [always] present for them. 36:75). Meaning: These gods they have taken cannot help these polytheists, for they possess nothing of good or evil.

As for His saying: وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ, it is apparent that the first of the two pronouns refers to the polytheists and the second to the gods besides Allah. The meaning is that the polytheists are an army (جند) for the gods. This is because one of the requirements of the meaning of "soldiery" (الجندية) is following and accompaniment, and it is the polytheists who are counted as followers of their gods, obeying them, not the other way around.

The meaning of "being present" (الإحضار) in His saying: مُحْضَرُونَ is being brought for recompense on the Day of Resurrection.
